- v1–2: so they may live and possess the land (this theme recurs throughout: 5:33; 8:1; 11:9; 16:20; 30:6, 16, culminating in 30:19–20)
- v3–4: historical example — disobedience at Beth Peor led to death; those who held fast to the Lord lived
- v5–8: obedience demonstrates to surrounding nations that they are God's people — there is a witness dimension that the New Testament picks up

**Q3. What makes the people of God unique in this section?**

The Lord is near to them as no other nation's god is near. The Lord has given them righteous laws like no other nation has. The Lord has made them wise and understanding in the sight of the nations. It is the word and nearness of the Lord that makes his people unique — not their size, power, or virtue.

---

### 4:9–14 — Remember How God Spoke

**Q4. What is the first part of the threefold warning?**

Be careful not to forget what they have seen. The danger is that those memories will 'fade from their heart' — not dramatic apostasy, but quiet, gradual slippage.

**Q5. What can they do to stop themselves from forgetting?**

Watch themselves carefully and teach what they have seen to their children and grandchildren. Remembering requires active, deliberate effort — it will not happen automatically.

**Q6. What in particular are they to remember? What did they see, and what did they hear? Why does this matter?**

At Horeb, they saw fire and cloud and darkness — but no form. They heard his word: the covenant, the Ten Commandments. The absence of any visible form is theologically decisive: making an image of God when you saw no form is not devotion, it is invention. They could not see God, and they should not try to represent him. He gave them his word, and that is what they must cling to.

---

### 4:15–22 — Be Careful Not to Slide into Idolatry

**Q7. What is the second warning? What danger are the people in?**

Warned not to become corrupt and make idols in any form — human, animal, bird, reptile, fish. The danger is being enticed to worship created things instead of the Creator. The comprehensive list of possible forms emphasises that no created thing is an adequate representation of God.

**Q8. How does Moses help them 'watch themselves carefully'?**

By reminding them of three things: (1) the Lord is sovereign and apportions things to the nations; (2) the Lord brought them out of Egypt — they belong to him by redemption; (3) even Moses himself was excluded from the land — no one is immune from the consequences of departing from God's word.

---

### 4:23–31 — Be Careful Not to Forget the Lord's Covenant; Even When You Do, He Will Remember

**Q9. What is the third part of the warning? What reason is given?**

Do not forget the Lord's covenant. The reason: 'the Lord your God is a consuming fire, a jealous God.' The same fire from which God spoke at Horeb will consume those who turn away. Jealousy here is covenant faithfulness — the appropriate response to betrayal, not petty insecurity.

**Q10. What does Moses warn will happen if they forget and turn to idolatry?**

They will be sent from the land and scattered among the nations — forced to worship idols made of wood and stone (v28), which is itself presented as part of the judgment, not just the consequence. Exile is the unmaking of everything the covenant had given them.

**Q11. What hope do we see in this section? What does it tell us about the Lord's character?**

If they repent and seek the Lord, he will hear them (vv29–31). He is merciful and will not abandon his people or forget his covenant. Even the announcement of judgment contains a door left open. This is the shape of grace throughout Scripture.

---

### 4:32–40 — There Is No God Like the Lord, So Obey His Word

**Q12. What has the Lord done that is utterly unlike anything other nations claim for their gods?**

He actually is the Creator. He has spoken clearly to his people from fire. He has taken one nation for himself from among all nations. He has done great and awesome deeds to save his people. He has given his word and been with them. He has chosen them and given them a land. The argument is cumulative and rhetorical: has any other god done any of this? No. Then worship him.

**Q13. What is Moses' closing appeal (vv39–40)?**

Acknowledge the utter uniqueness of the Lord and obey his word — so that they and their children may be blessed with long life in the land. The appeal is both theological (acknowledge who he is) and practical (obey him). The two cannot be separated.

---

### 4:41–43 — Cities of Refuge

*(Don't spend long on this. The fuller treatment comes in ch 19. A brief note is sufficient.)*

The cities of refuge are a concrete example of the righteous laws just described: they value human life, distinguish between deliberate and accidental killing, ensure justice for the accused, and prevent the cycle of blood vengeance. They are law in the service of people, not law as an abstraction.

---

### Application Questions

*(Use the handout questions to guide small group discussion.)*

**What did the people do at Beth Peor and why? What was the Lord's response?**

They worshipped Baal and engaged in sexual immorality with Moabite women — following the gods of the nations around them at the point of greatest vulnerability (prosperity and proximity). The Lord's response was severe: plague and death. Idolatry is not a private matter; it has community consequences.

**How can we be tempted to drift from God's word?**

Encourage honest, specific answers. Drifting from Scripture often happens gradually and imperceptibly — not through dramatic rejection but through neglect, distraction, or the slow gravitational pull of other priorities.

**What idolatry is likely to tempt us as Christians? Read Hebrews 12:18–29. How can this help us avoid turning away from the Lord?**

Hebrews applies the Sinai imagery directly to Christian readers: we have come to a better mountain, with a better mediator, and we must not refuse him who speaks. The warning is the same as Deuteronomy's, intensified. The functional idols that compete with God today are typically good things elevated to ultimate things: security, status, family, comfort, control.

**What are we called to remember as Christians? How can we apply the warnings of Deuteronomy 4 to ourselves as New Testament believers?**

We are called to remember the cross and resurrection — the even greater act of redemption that grounds our obedience. The discipline of active remembering (rehearsing what God has done, teaching it to the next generation, returning to it in prayer and worship) is as necessary for us as it was for Israel.

**Deuteronomy 4 reminds us that the Lord is God and there is no other. What does it mean for us to reflect on the uniqueness of the Lord revealed in Christ?**

The series of rhetorical questions in vv32–34 can be reframed for a Christian audience: Has any other religion given a God who entered his own creation, died for his people's sin, and rose again? The answer shapes everything.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 4:44–5:33: Walk in the Way the Lord Your God Has Commanded

background.body:
**The transition to Moses' second sermon.** Chapter 4:44–49 is an editorial introduction marking the beginning of Moses' second and longest sermon (5:1–28:68). This sermon is the heart of the book — it begins with the Ten Commandments and then unpacks their implications across a vast range of life situations. This session covers the framing of the Commandments: the context in which they were given, the character of the God who gave them, and the role of Moses as mediator.

**The context of the Ten Commandments.** The Commandments were given at Horeb (Sinai), in the aftermath of the exodus, to a people surrounded by pagan nations. They were not given to help people earn salvation; they were given to a people already redeemed, to show them how to live as the people of the God who had saved them. This order — redemption first, then law — is foundational and must not be reversed.

**The covenant at Horeb (5:3).** Moses makes the striking claim that the covenant was made 'not with our ancestors but with us, with all of us who are alive here today.' This is not a denial of historical continuity but an assertion of contemporaneity: each generation stands personally before God's word. The covenant is not an heirloom to be inherited; it is a living reality to be entered into afresh.

**Moses as mediator (5:23–31).** The people's terror at God's voice is not weakness — it is an accurate instinct. A holy God speaking directly to sinful people is genuinely dangerous. Their request for a mediator is not faithlessness; God commends it. Moses stands between God and the people, receives the word, and delivers it. This is a pattern of profound theological significance, pointing forward to the ultimate mediator.

**The purpose of the commands (5:32–33).** The section closes with a clear statement of purpose: obedience is not an end in itself. It is the path to life, to flourishing in the land God has given. The commands are not a burden; they are the shape of a blessed life.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: walk in the way the Lord your God has commanded you.** This is the summary of the entire second sermon before it has even begun. Everything that follows in chapters 5–28 is an unpacking of this one imperative.

**Redemption precedes law.** The Commandments begin: 'I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of Egypt, out of the land of slavery' (5:6). God introduces himself as Redeemer before he gives a single command. The law is given within a relationship already established by grace. This is not a new point for Christians, but it bears dwelling on — it is the structure of the gospel.

**The covenant is contemporary.** 'Not with our ancestors… but with us' (5:3). Moses insists that this covenant is not something that happened to a previous generation and has since been passed down. Each person standing before him is a direct party to it. That contemporaneity applies equally to those reading Deuteronomy today.

**The Ten Commandments as a vision of human flourishing.** Rather than treating the Commandments as a list of prohibitions to be avoided, help the group see them as a positive vision — of what a community shaped by love for God and neighbour actually looks like. Each commandment, approached from the positive direction, describes something beautiful.

**Moses as the pattern of the mediator.** The people cannot stand in the direct presence of God's holiness and survive. They need someone to stand in the gap, receive the word, and deliver it. Moses performs that function here, and explicitly: 'I stood between the Lord and you' (5:5). The New Testament identifies Jesus as the one who fulfils that role finally and completely (Hebrews 1:1–2; 1 Timothy 2:5).

pastorals.body:
**People have very different relationships with the Ten Commandments.** Some will have grown up with them as a kind of moral backdrop — vaguely familiar, not deeply considered. Some will have been taught them in a legalistic context and carry a burden of condemnation. Some will be encountering them seriously for the first time. Calibrate your introduction accordingly.

**The Sabbath commandment is a perennial flashpoint.** How Christians relate to the Sabbath is genuinely contested, and your group will likely have a range of views. The PDF notes suggest engaging with Hebrews 4 as a way of thinking about Sabbath rest in a Christian frame. Acknowledge the diversity of Christian practice and keep the focus on the theological principle — rest as trust, rest as reflection of God's own rhythm — rather than getting drawn into debates about Sunday trading.

**The terror of the people (5:25–27) is a pastoral gift.** It names something that people rarely say out loud: encountering the holy God is genuinely frightening. If anyone in your group is working through fear in relation to God — whether fear of judgment, fear of being truly known, or fear of what obedience might cost — this passage can open a conversation about why a mediator is not a weakness but a mercy.

exegetical.body:
**'Not with our ancestors but with us' (5:3).** This verse has puzzled readers: clearly the people present had ancestors who were at Horeb. The point is not chronological but existential — Moses is not allowing his audience to treat the covenant as something that applies to someone else. The same move is made in Christian preaching when the gospel is addressed to the present congregation, not just to the original recipients.

**The Sabbath rationale in Deuteronomy vs Exodus.** In Exodus 20:11, the Sabbath is grounded in creation (God rested on the seventh day). In Deuteronomy 5:15, it is grounded in redemption (remember that you were slaves in Egypt). Both grounds are given in different contexts. The Deuteronomy grounding is pastoral and social: the Sabbath is a reminder of freedom. Slaves do not get to rest; free people do. The command to let servants rest is therefore an extension of the exodus logic.

**'I stood between the Lord and you' (5:5).** The Hebrew verb translated 'stood between' (*'amad bên*) is the language of mediation and intercession. Moses is not merely a messenger; he is a buffer, a representative, one who bears the weight of the encounter so others can receive the word safely. This is the theological heartbeat of the passage.

**'Walk in all the way the Lord your God has commanded you' (5:33).** The singular 'way' (*haderekh*) is significant. Obedience in Deuteronomy is not a fragmented list of individual rules; it is a single path, a whole manner of life. The image of walking recurs throughout the book and is taken up extensively in the New Testament.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the group spending all its time on the content of individual commandments and missing the frame around them — why God gave them, to whom, and with what goal. The commandments are important, but this session's particular contribution is the *context* of the commandments: given by a Redeemer, received through a mediator, aimed at a flourishing life. Keep coming back to that frame.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The theological frame of redemption-before-law, the contemporaneity of the covenant, Moses as mediator, and the purpose of the commands are covered in the sections above. The notes below guide the group through the text.)*

---

### 4:44–5:5 and 5:23–33 — The Context of the Ten Commandments

**Q1. Moses reminds the Israelites of the commandments God had given them. What was the context?**

Surrounded by pagan nations; after being saved from Egypt; at Horeb; given within a covenant relationship. The commandments did not drop from the sky — they were embedded in a specific historical relationship between a specific God and a specific people he had redeemed.

**Q2. Look at 5:3. What is the significance of this, given that those listening to Moses would have been young children or not yet born at the time of Horeb? What does this tell us about God's covenant?**

The covenant is not merely historical — it is contemporary. Moses refuses to allow his audience to treat it as something that happened to someone else. Every generation stands directly before God's word. This is a deliberately destabilising move: you cannot hide behind your ancestors.

**Q3. Look at 5:25–31. How do the people respond to the voice of the Lord? What does this tell us about humanity's ability to relate directly to God? What does God's response teach us? How does Moses point us to Jesus here?**

Terror. The people genuinely believe they will die if they continue to hear God's voice directly — and they are not wrong in their instinct. God's response is remarkable: he commends their request for a mediator ('they are right in what they have said', v28) and agrees to speak through Moses. God provides what sinful humans need to have any relationship with him at all. Moses points forward to Jesus, our ultimate mediator (Hebrews 1:1–2; 1 Timothy 2:5).

**Q4. What do 5:32–33 tell us about the purpose of God's covenant and commands?**

God wants to bless his people in the land he has given them. Obedience leads to blessing — not as mechanical cause and effect, but because the commands describe the shape of life with God, and life with God is the fullest kind of life there is.

---

### 5:6–22 — The Ten Commandments

*(This section is worked through in small groups using the handout. The leader should read through the Commandments in advance and be prepared to help groups engage with each one at the level of: what is commanded/prohibited; what rationale is given; what does this reveal about what God values; and what does positive obedience look like for us.)*

**Key notes for the leader by commandment:**

**1st commandment (v7):** No other gods. The reason is given in v6: 'I am the Lord your God who brought you out of Egypt.' The ground of exclusive loyalty is exclusive redemption. Positive obedience means treating the Lord as the only ultimate source of security, identity, and worth.

**2nd commandment (vv8–10):** No images. See exegetical and background notes above. The jealousy of God (v9) is covenant jealousy — the refusal to share what belongs to an exclusive relationship. The multi-generational consequences (v9–10) are a reminder that idolatry is never purely personal.

**3rd commandment (v11):** Do not misuse the Lord's name. This is broader than swearing — it includes using God's name to give weight to false claims, invoking him to legitimise what he does not legitimise, or treating him casually.

**4th commandment (vv12–15):** Observe the Sabbath. Note the Deuteronomy rationale: remember you were slaves in Egypt. Sabbath is about freedom, not productivity. The command to let servants rest is an extension of exodus logic into daily life.

**5th commandment (v16):** Honour father and mother. The only commandment with a promise attached. Positive obedience includes caring for ageing parents, valuing wisdom across generations, and not treating the elderly as burdens.

**6th–9th commandments (vv17–20):** Do not murder, commit adultery, steal, give false testimony. These protect the foundations of communal life: life, marriage, property, truth. Jesus radicalises all of them inward in the Sermon on the Mount.

**10th commandment (v21):** Do not covet. Unique in legislating the interior life. What we desire shapes what we do; covetousness is the engine of most other sins.

---

### Application — Jesus and the Ten Commandments

*(Using the handout passages.)*

**Mark 2:23–28:** Jesus is Lord of the Sabbath — he fulfils what the Sabbath pointed to, and he has authority to interpret its application.

**Matthew 5:21–26:** Murder begins with anger and contempt. Jesus drives the sixth commandment inward to its root.

**Matthew 5:27–30:** Adultery begins with lust. Same move: the commandment addresses the heart, not just the hand.

**Mark 7:9–13:** The fifth commandment is violated when religious tradition is used as an excuse to avoid caring for parents. Jesus exposes the self-serving abuse of law.

**Matthew 10:37–38 (application of the first commandment):** Love for Jesus must exceed love for family — the first commandment in its ultimate form. This is likely to provoke discussion. Help the group sit with it rather than rushing to soften it.

**What are the most likely idols we might be tempted to worship?**

Encourage specific, honest engagement. Common answers: security (money, status), relationships (family, romance), achievement, comfort, reputation. The question is not whether these things are good, but whether they function as ultimate.

**Positive obedience to the Ten Commandments — what might it look like?**

This is a generative question. Positive obedience is not just avoiding the prohibited thing but actively pursuing its opposite: not just not murdering, but actively protecting and valuing life; not just not stealing, but actively giving; not just not lying, but actively pursuing and speaking truth.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 6: Love the Loving Lord with Everything

background.body:
**Position in the sermon.** Chapter 6 immediately follows the Ten Commandments and functions as their interpretive key. If chapter 5 gives the commands, chapter 6 gives the heart from which all obedience must flow: love for the Lord. Chapters 7–9 then expand on what loving the Lord with heart, soul, and strength looks like in three specific arenas: not compromising with the world around you (ch 7), remembering the Lord's provision even in prosperity (ch 8), and seeking God's righteousness while avoiding rebellion (ch 9–10).

**The Shema (6:4–5).** These two verses constitute the *Shema* (from the Hebrew word for 'hear'), the central confession of Jewish faith, recited twice daily by observant Jews to this day. The declaration 'The Lord our God, the Lord is one' is an assertion of the exclusive and unified character of Israel's God in a world of many competing deities. The response it demands — love with all heart, soul, and strength — is total, not partial. Jesus identifies this as the greatest commandment (Mark 12:28–34).

**The three challenges to love (6:10–19).** Moses anticipates three specific threats to wholehearted love for the Lord once the people are in the land: prosperity (they may become complacent and forget the Lord); conformity (they may adopt the gods of the nations around them); and hardship (they may doubt God's goodness and test him, as at Massah — see Exodus 17:1–7). These three threats are not random; they cover the main ways in which comfortable, established life erodes religious seriousness.

**The Massah incident (6:16).** At Massah, the people tested the Lord by demanding water and questioning whether he was among them at all — despite having witnessed the plagues and the exodus. The reference is a compressed warning: don't repeat that pattern of demanding proof when God has already demonstrated his faithfulness abundantly.

**Love as response (6:20–25).** The chapter closes with an instruction about how to answer children who ask why the commands exist. The answer is not 'because God said so' but 'because of what God did.' Love and obedience are grounded in gratitude for salvation. Any other grounding will eventually fail.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: love the loving Lord with everything, through good times and bad.** This is not just one command among many — it is the command from which all others flow. Jesus says so explicitly (Mark 12:29–31). Help the group feel the weight of its centrality.

**The Lord is one — and therefore demands one thing.** The logic of the Shema is tight: because God is one (not divided, not competing with himself, not one option among many), the response to him must be undivided. Partial love for the one God is a contradiction in terms. The radical totalness of 'heart, soul, and strength' is not arbitrary intensity; it follows from the nature of who God is.

**Love is not just feeling — it is obedience, memory, and teaching.** Verses 6–9 describe love in strikingly practical terms: keep these words on your heart, teach them to your children, talk about them constantly, bind them to yourself, write them on your doorframes. Love for God is expressed and sustained through the discipline of keeping his word central to daily life.

**Prosperity is a more dangerous test than hardship.** Moses devotes more attention to the threat of prosperity than to any other challenge in this chapter. The danger is not dramatic apostasy but quiet forgetting — settling into blessing and gradually losing sight of the one who gave it. This is worth pressing on: it is likely the most relevant threat for many in your group.

**All our love for God is a response to his love for us.** Verses 20–25 make explicit what has been implicit throughout: obedience flows from gratitude. 'We love because he first loved us' (1 John 4:19) is not a New Testament innovation — it is already the structure of Deuteronomy. God saved first; we respond.

pastorals.body:
**'Love the Lord your God' can sound both obvious and impossible.** Some in your group will feel they do love God — and will be challenged by the 'with everything' qualifier. Others will feel they don't love God enough and will be discouraged. Both responses are worth addressing: the command is not a description of our current state but a call toward it, and it is a call that can only be answered in dependence on God himself (see 30:6 — 'the Lord your God will circumcise your hearts').

**The prosperity challenge is personally sensitive.** Asking people to examine whether prosperity has made them spiritually complacent requires trust in the group. Some may be in genuine material difficulty, for whom this feels irrelevant. Others may be prospering and feel implicitly accused. Frame the question carefully: Moses is not saying prosperity is bad, but that it creates a specific spiritual vulnerability that requires active attention.

**Hardship and testing God.** The reference to Massah (v16) opens the door to a conversation about people who are in hard times and genuinely struggling to trust God. Do not shut this down in the name of application — let the question breathe. The point is not that questioning God is always wrong, but that demanding proof while refusing to look at the evidence is a form of faithlessness.

exegetical.body:
**'The Lord our God, the Lord is one' (6:4).** The Hebrew is famously ambiguous and has been rendered in several ways: 'The Lord is our God, the Lord alone'; 'The Lord our God is one Lord'; 'The Lord our God, the Lord is one.' The most likely sense combines both unity and exclusivity: there is only one Lord, and he is ours. The Christian reading adds that the 'one' God is Trinitarian — Father, Son, and Spirit — without reducing the force of the exclusive claim.

**Heart, soul, and strength (6:5).** In Hebrew anthropology, *heart* (*lev*) refers primarily to the mind and will, not the emotions. *Soul* (*nephesh*) is the whole self, the living being. *Strength* (*me'od*) is intensity or resources. Together they cover every dimension of human existence. The point is totality, not a taxonomy of three parts. When Jesus adds 'mind' (Mark 12:30), he is not correcting Moses; he is clarifying for a Greek-influenced audience.

**'Tie them as symbols on your hands… on your foreheads… write them on your doorframes' (6:8–9).** Observant Jews took these instructions literally, producing tefillin (phylacteries) and mezuzot. The original intent was probably metaphorical — a way of saying 'let these words permeate everything: action (hands), thought (forehead), home (doorframe).' Both interpretations honour the text's intent. Don't dismiss the literal practice, but don't require it either.

**'Do not put the Lord your God to the test' (6:16).** Jesus quotes this verse in the wilderness temptation (Matthew 4:7) when Satan invites him to demand a dramatic demonstration of God's protection. Testing God is treating him as a means to our ends — demanding he prove himself on our terms rather than trusting what he has already shown.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the discussion of 'loving God with everything' becoming either a guilt exercise or an abstract aspiration. The most useful move is to keep asking: what would this actually look like this week, in your specific circumstances? Love for God expressed in heart, soul, and strength is not a feeling to be cultivated in private — it is a whole-of-life orientation that shows up in decisions, habits, conversations, and what we do with money and time.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The theology of the Shema, the three threats to love, and the responsive character of all obedience are covered in the sections above. The notes below guide the group through the passage.)*

---

### 6:1–3 — Moses' Exhortation

**Q1. Moses calls the Israelites to obey God's commands (referring back to ch 5). What reasons does he give?**

- That they and their children may fear the Lord
- That they may enjoy long life
- That it may go well with them
- That they may increase greatly

The reasons are relational and communal, not merely individual. Obedience shapes not just the individual but the generations that follow.

---

### 6:4–9 — Love the Lord Your God with Everything

**Q2. What characteristic of the Lord is impressed upon the Israelites here? Why? What are the implications?**

Oneness. The Lord is the unique, singular Lord — not one deity among competitors, not a divided or inconsistent being, but the one God. Because God is one, the response to him must be undivided love with every part of the person. You cannot give partial allegiance to the one God; it is a contradiction.

**Q3. How are God's people to love him? What does this mean?**

Wholeheartedly, with everything — heart, soul, and strength. Draw out from the exegetical notes that 'heart' here is primarily the mind and will, not the emotions. This is not a command to feel a particular way; it is a command to orient the whole self — thought, will, and resources — toward the Lord.

**Q4. Read Mark 12:28–34. How does Jesus speak of these verses? Why is this the 'greatest commandment'?**

Jesus identifies it as the greatest commandment because everything else hangs from it. All obedience to God springs from love for God, and love for God is grounded in his character and his actions. It is easy to obey rules formulaically; it is much harder — and much better — to love. If love for the Lord comes first, the rest will follow.

**Q5. How do vv6–9 relate to vv4–5? How are they a practical response?**

If the Lord is the one and only God, and if his people are to love him wholeheartedly, his word will be central to their lives — in thought, conversation, teaching, home, and activity. The practical instructions of vv6–9 are not legalism; they are love expressed through the discipline of keeping God's word constantly present.

---

### 6:10–19 — Three Things to Challenge Your Love

**Q6. What does Moses predict is likely to challenge their love for the Lord? (See Exodus 17:1–7 for the Massah incident.)**

- **Prosperity (vv10–12):** They may become complacent and forget the Lord who gave them everything they now enjoy. The danger is not ingratitude as a moral failure but as a spiritual drift — the slow eclipse of God by his own gifts.
- **Conformity (vv13–15):** They may turn to the gods of the nations around them, following the path of least resistance when surrounded by alternative ways of life.
- **Hardship (v16):** They may face tough times, doubt the Lord's goodness, and test him — demanding proof of his presence as Israel did at Massah, despite having abundant evidence already.

**Q7. What guards against these challenges does Moses give?**

- Do not forget (active, disciplined remembering)
- The warning of God's jealousy and judgment for those who follow other gods
- Do what is right and live long — obedience as the path to life, not as an arbitrary requirement

---

### 6:20–25 — Love Him Because He First Loved You

**Q8. How do vv20–25 relate to the theme of loving the Lord? What does active, intentional remembering look like, and why is it so important?**

The Lord saved his people — brought them out of Egypt, gave them the land, gave them his commands. Any love we have for him is a response to his prior love. Even his commands are an act of love: they are the shape of a flourishing life, given by the God who wants his people to flourish. Intentional remembering — telling the story of salvation to the next generation, returning to it in worship, allowing it to reframe present experience — keeps love from growing cold.

---

### Application Questions

**What does it look like to love the Lord with everything in our context? Think of both emotional and practical expressions of love.**

Encourage specificity. Emotional: genuine delight in God, gratitude in prayer, worship that engages the whole person. Practical: financial decisions that reflect trust in God rather than anxiety; time that is ordered around what God values; conversations in which God's word is genuinely present; relationships that prioritise love for neighbour as an expression of love for God.

**How might prosperity, conformity, or hardship challenge our love for the Lord? What might that look like?**

Prosperity: busyness replacing prayer; comfort reducing dependence; success creating self-sufficiency. Conformity: adopting the values of surrounding culture in areas of sexuality, money, or ambition without noticing. Hardship: prayer becoming transactional ('if you help me I will believe'); doubt hardening into accusation.

**How can we encourage each other to remember the Lord's salvation and love him?**

This is a practical, community-forming question. Encourage concrete answers: regular Scripture reading, sharing testimonies, celebrating what God has done, holding one another accountable in love, marking occasions of answered prayer.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 7: Do Not Compromise — Live Distinctively as a Holy People

background.body:
**Position in the sermon.** Chapter 7 is the first expansion of the Shema's call to wholehearted love (6:5). Loving the Lord with everything looks like, first, refusing to compromise with the world's alternative loyalties. The chapter moves from the specific historical command to destroy the Canaanite nations and their worship to the underlying theological principle: the people of God are set apart for the Lord, and anything that threatens that distinctiveness must be taken with deadly seriousness.

**The command to destroy the nations.** This is one of the most difficult passages in Deuteronomy for modern readers. A few points of orientation are important for the leader: (1) The command applies specifically and uniquely to the Canaanite nations in a specific historical moment — it is not a general licence for holy war. (2) The reason given is theological and pastoral: the Canaanites' practices were corrupt and contagious, and intermarriage and treaty would result in Israel being drawn into idolatry. (3) The command is already being partially spiritualised within Deuteronomy: the focus quickly shifts from physical destruction to the destruction of idols and false worship (vv5, 25–26). (4) Moses knows Israel will not fully obey this command — the rest of the Old Testament shows it. The command functions as an urgent, idealistic call to radical holiness, not a simple military instruction.

**The election of Israel (7:6–11).** The reason for Israel's election is stated with striking clarity: not because they were great or numerous — they were the smallest of peoples — but because of the Lord's love and his faithfulness to his covenant with their ancestors. Election is grounded entirely in God's character, not Israel's merit. This is as important for what it rules out as for what it asserts.

**Baal and Ashtoreth (7:13).** These are the principal deities of Canaanite religion — Baal the storm and harvest god, Ashtoreth the fertility goddess. Worshipping them was the culturally expected way to ensure agricultural prosperity. Moses' point is that the blessings Israel might seek from these gods — grain, wine, oil, livestock, children — come from the Lord alone. There is no need to look elsewhere, and looking elsewhere is both irrational and faithless.

**Fear as the antidote to idolatry (7:17–26).** Moses anticipates that the Israelites will be intimidated by the strength of the nations. His response is not to minimise the threat but to reframe it: the question is not whether the nations are strong, but who is fighting. If the Lord is for Israel, the nations' strength is irrelevant.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: do not compromise with the world around you out of fear, but live distinctively as a people holy to the Lord.** Holiness in Deuteronomy is not primarily about moral perfection; it is about belonging — being set apart for the Lord, defined by relationship with him rather than by the world around.

**Idolatry is contagious.** The reason for the severity of the command to destroy is not tribal hostility but spiritual infection. Moses is not portraying the Canaanites as racially inferior; he is describing the contagious power of false worship. Idolatry spreads through relationship, assimilation, and the gradual normalisation of competing loyalties. This dynamic is as active in any culture as it was in Canaan.

**Election is about God's character, not Israel's greatness.** 'The Lord did not set his affection on you and choose you because you were more numerous… but because the Lord loved you' (7:7–8). This is one of the most theologically important statements in the book. God's choice of Israel — and, by extension, of the church — is an act of pure grace that cannot be accounted for by anything in the recipients. It should produce humility, not pride.

**Trust in God's provision makes compromise unnecessary.** The nations around Israel worship Baal for harvest and Ashtoreth for fertility — because they are afraid of what will happen if they don't. Israel is told that everything they need comes from the Lord. If they truly believe that, the appeal of the alternative gods evaporates. Fear drives compromise; trust enables distinctiveness.

**The antidote to the fear of enemies is the memory of what God has done.** Moses does not tell the Israelites not to be afraid by minimising the danger. He tells them to remember Egypt (v18). The same God who broke the most powerful empire in the world will deal with the Canaanites. Evidence-based trust is the answer to fear-based compromise.

pastorals.body:
**The command to destroy the nations will be disturbing for some.** Handle this with honesty rather than either dismissal or forced resolution. The key moves are: (1) acknowledge the difficulty; (2) situate the command historically and theologically (see background); (3) note that the underlying principle — that idolatry must not be accommodated — is restated throughout the New Testament, even if the specific application changes completely. You are not required to resolve every theological question about the conquest; you are required to keep the group engaged with the text.

**The language of being God's 'treasured possession' (7:6) can be extraordinarily powerful.** For people who feel of little worth — overlooked, dismissed, made to feel small — the claim that the God of the universe chooses and treasures them is not sentimentality; it is a profound counter-statement to everything the world has told them. Give this room if it lands.

**The question of compromise is personally sensitive.** People in mixed-faith families, people navigating workplaces with significant ethical pressures, people in communities where Christian distinctiveness has a social cost — all of these will feel the weight of this chapter differently. Don't flatten those complexities, but do help the group see that the call to distinctiveness is not optional and is not about condemnation of the world, but about loyalty to the Lord.

exegetical.body:
**'Set apart as holy' (*qadosh*, 7:6).** The root meaning of holiness in Hebrew is separation or distinction — being marked off for a particular purpose or person. It is not primarily a moral category (though moral implications follow). Israel is holy because she belongs to the Lord. The application to the church is direct: 'a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation' (1 Peter 2:9).

**'No treaties, no mercy, no intermarriage' (7:2–3).** The severity here is pastoral, not ethnic. The specific danger of intermarriage is spelled out in v4: it will lead the Israelites to follow other gods. Moses has seen it happen already — at Beth Peor, where intermingling with Moabite women led directly to the worship of Baal. The command is about protecting the people from a known and immediate spiritual danger.

**'Detestable' (*to'evah*, 7:25–26).** This is a strong term in Deuteronomy, used particularly for things associated with idolatry. The Israelites are not to bring detestable things into their homes — even the gold and silver of destroyed idols, which might seem harmless enough. The association with idolatry makes them contagious. The principle: you cannot neutralise idolatry by repackaging it.

**'The Lord will drive out those nations… little by little' (7:22).** The conquest will not be instantaneous — partly to prevent the land becoming overrun with wild animals. This realistic note is important: God's faithfulness does not mean his purposes unfold on a human timetable. Patience is part of trust.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the application discussion being pulled toward abstract principle and away from specific, personal territory. 'Compromise with the world' can be discussed at a comfortable distance. The harder questions are: where *specifically* am I most vulnerable to accommodating something that competes with wholehearted love for the Lord? What relationship, habit, ambition, or comfort is functioning as a Canaanite god in my life? Push the group gently toward that level of specificity.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The historical and theological context of the conquest command, the theology of election, and the pastoral dimensions of distinctiveness and fear are covered in the sections above. The notes below guide the group through the passage.)*

---

### 7:1–5 — Do Not Compromise

**Q1. How are the nations in the land described? What are they like?**

Larger and stronger than the Israelites. Idolatrous — many false gods. They do terrible things that the Lord hates (see also 12:31). The description is designed to make the challenge feel real and the call to distinctive holiness feel urgent.

**Q2. What are the Israelites to do, and why?**

Destroy them utterly. No treaties, no mercy, no intermarriage — because these nations will lead Israel into idolatry, which will result in destruction. They are to be holy, set apart, the Lord's treasured possession. The reason for the severity is not ethnic but theological: spiritual infection is the threat.

**Q3. What does this suggest about the nature of idolatry?**

Contagious, enticing, and destructive. It spreads through relationship and assimilation. It is something the Lord utterly hates and which, if left unchecked, will lead to total ruin. God will judge idolaters.

---

### 7:6–11 — The Lord Chose You

**Q4. Why was Israel chosen? Why was she not chosen?**

Because of the Lord's love and faithfulness to his covenant with the patriarchs. Explicitly not because Israel was more numerous — she was the smallest of peoples. The grounds of election are entirely in God's character, not Israel's. This should produce humility and gratitude, not pride.

**Q5. What does this tell us about the character of God? How will it help them as they enter the land?**

God is faithful and keeps his word. He chose Israel not because of anything in her, so she cannot claim credit for the choice — but she can trust that the same faithfulness that drove the choice will sustain it. He will keep his word to bless those who love him and to judge those who hate him. They can trust him to deliver the nations into their hands.

---

### 7:12–16 — The Lord Provides for You

**Q6. Why does Moses remind them of the blessings God has promised if they obey? How will this protect them against compromise?**

Reminder of God's love and promise of provision. Everything they need to thrive comes from the Lord — grain, wine, oil, livestock, children. The nations around them worship Baal and Ashtoreth to get these things, because they are afraid of what will happen if they don't. But those things come from the Lord alone. Obedient trust in him is the way to blessing. If Israel truly believes that, the appeal of the other gods disappears.

---

### 7:17–26 — Do Not Fear, but Destroy False Worship

**Q7. What might the Israelites be afraid of?**

The strength of the other nations — that they won't be able to drive them out. The fear is realistic: some of these nations really are stronger.

**Q8. What is the antidote to this fear?**

Remembering what the Lord has already done — bringing them out of Egypt, dealing with Pharaoh — and what he has promised to do. The battle belongs to the Lord, not to them. He will deliver the nations to them and make them unable to withstand Israel. The antidote to fear is not bravado; it is memory of God's track record.

**Q9. What is to be done with the idols, and why?**

Utterly destroyed. Even the gold and silver should not be taken — it is detestable to the Lord and will ensnare them. All the idols must be totally destroyed so as not to tempt God's people away from him and prevent them from being a holy people set apart for the Lord.

---

### Application Questions

**Where are we most vulnerable to compromise, theologically and morally? What is there in the world around us to entice us away from wholehearted love for Jesus?**

Encourage honest, specific engagement. Common areas: sexual ethics, money and materialism, the pursuit of social approval, syncretistic spirituality (combining Christianity with other frameworks), the gradual adoption of surrounding culture's assumptions about what constitutes a good life.

**Read Mark 9:43–48. How does Deuteronomy 7 help us understand the seriousness of sin and how Christians are expected to view it?**

Jesus uses the same logic as Moses: if something is causing you to sin, deal with it radically. The hyperbole of cutting off hand or foot serves the same function as Moses' drastic commands — it communicates that nothing is worth compromising your relationship with God. Deuteronomy 7 gives us the Old Testament grounding for that seriousness.

**What did it mean for Israel to be the Lord's treasured possession? What does it mean for us? (1 Peter 2:9–10)**

For Israel: chosen, holy, set apart, belonging entirely to the Lord — defined by him rather than by the surrounding culture. For us: the same categories, applied to the church through Christ. 'Once you were not a people, but now you are the people of God.' The status is a gift; the call to distinctiveness follows from it.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 8: The Lord Disciplines His Children — Remember His Provision and Beware of Prosperity

background.body:
**Position in the sermon.** Chapter 8 is the second expansion of the Shema's call to wholehearted love (6:5). Where chapter 7 addressed the danger of compromise through conformity and fear, chapter 8 addresses the danger of prosperity. This is the spiritually subtler threat — not outright apostasy but gradual self-sufficiency, not abandoning God dramatically but slowly forgetting him as the source of everything.

**The wilderness as school (8:2–5).** Moses reframes the forty years of wilderness wandering, which in chapters 1–3 was presented primarily as punishment for rebellion, now as a period of divine education. God humbled the people, tested them, fed them with manna, and sustained them — not out of indifference but as a father disciplines a son. The wilderness was not a detour; it was a curriculum.

**Manna and the word of God (8:3).** The provision of manna is interpreted by Moses in a way that goes beyond its immediate significance. The people needed to learn that they do not live 'by bread alone' but 'by every word that comes from the mouth of the Lord.' Their physical dependence on God for daily food was a training exercise in deeper dependence — on his word, his provision, and his presence. Jesus quotes this verse in his wilderness temptation (Matthew 4:4), identifying himself as the one who truly and fully lives by the Father's word.

**The good land (8:7–10).** Moses' description of the land is lyrical and deliberate — water everywhere, wheat and barley, vines and fig trees, pomegranates and olive oil and honey, iron and copper in the hills. It is a picture of abundance in every category. The point is not to generate excitement but to set up the warning: when you have all this, will you remember who gave it?

**The spiritual danger of prosperity (8:11–20).** The movement Moses traces is precise: full stomachs → comfortable homes → growing herds and silver and gold → proud heart → forgetting God → taking credit for your own wealth → destruction. This is not a hypothetical; Moses knows it will happen. The warning is the more urgent because the threat is invisible — prosperity does not announce itself as a spiritual danger.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: the Lord disciplines his children, so remember his provision and beware of prosperity.** This is one of the most practically relevant chapters in Deuteronomy for materially comfortable Western (or indeed any prosperous) Christians.

**Hardship can be formative, not merely punitive.** The wilderness was punishment for rebellion — but it was also a school. God used the experience to humble the people, to expose their dependence on him, and to test whether they would trust him when things were difficult. The reframing of suffering as discipline rather than mere punishment is theologically important and pastorally significant.

**'You do not live on bread alone' (8:3).** Physical need is a parable of deeper need. The manna taught Israel that their fundamental dependence was not on food but on God's word. The lesson is not that food doesn't matter, but that everything material points beyond itself to the God who gives it. This is the theological ground for a sacramental view of everyday life.

**Prosperity creates the illusion of self-sufficiency.** The specific danger Moses identifies in vv17–18 is the thought: 'My power and the strength of my hands have produced this wealth for me.' It is not that the prosperous person denies God; they simply stop thinking about him as the source. The gift eclipses the giver. This is subtle enough that people rarely notice it happening.

**Remembering is the antidote to forgetting — and it must be deliberate.** Moses commands them to remember (v18) as the counterweight to the danger of forgetting (v11, 14, 19). This is not passive nostalgia; it is active, disciplined rehearsal of what God has done. The discipline of gratitude is the spiritual practice that keeps prosperity from becoming a spiritual prison.

pastorals.body:
**This chapter will cut differently depending on the material circumstances of those in your group.** For those who are genuinely struggling financially, the warning about prosperity may feel irrelevant or even tone-deaf. For those who are comfortable, it may feel uncomfortably pointed. Hold both groups in view: the principle of trusting God for provision applies in both directions — in scarcity and in plenty — and the discipline of gratitude matters in both circumstances.

**The language of discipline (8:5) requires care.** The analogy of a father disciplining his son is meant to be comforting — it reframes suffering as purposive rather than arbitrary, as an expression of love rather than abandonment. But for people with experience of harsh or abusive parenting, the analogy can land badly. Be attentive to this and, if needed, distinguish between godly discipline (which is always purposive, measured, and loving) and human abuse.

**Pride is hard to confess.** The spiritual danger Moses identifies — 'my power and my strength have produced this' — is not something people readily admit to. The discussion will go deeper if you model honesty yourself, or ask a concrete question: when did you last attribute your success to your own effort rather than to God's provision? What does that reveal?

exegetical.body:
**'He humbled you, causing you to hunger' (8:3).** The connection between humbling and hunger is precise: hunger exposes dependence. A well-fed person can believe, at least functionally, that they are self-sufficient. A hungry person cannot. The manna was given after the hunger, not before — so that the lesson of dependence would be felt before the provision arrived.

**'As a man disciplines his son' (8:5).** The Hebrew word for discipline (*yasar*) covers a range from instruction to correction to punishment, all with the goal of formation. The father-son analogy is significant: it locates suffering within a relationship of love and purpose, not within an arbitrary exercise of power. The same analogy is taken up in Hebrews 12:5–11.

**'Remember the Lord your God, for it is he who gives you the ability to produce wealth' (8:18).** This is a striking economic theology. The capacity to work, the skills deployed, the opportunities taken — all of these are themselves gifts from God. There is no level at which human achievement is purely self-generated. This does not negate human agency or effort; it locates them within a framework of gift and dependence.

**'As he swore to your ancestors' (8:18).** The purpose of wealth-giving is covenantal: God enables the production of wealth 'to confirm his covenant.' Prosperity is not an end in itself; it is a means of demonstrating God's faithfulness to his word. The same principle applies to the church: material blessing is always in the service of something larger than personal comfort.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the discussion of prosperity and pride becoming moralistic — a general agreement that materialism is bad, followed by little personal application. The most useful intervention is to ask: not 'is materialism a problem in general?' but 'where specifically do I see the pattern Moses describes playing out in my own life?' The sin of self-sufficiency is rarely dramatic; it tends to look like a full schedule, a comfortable routine, and less and less felt need for God. Naming that concretely is more fruitful than condemning abstract consumerism.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The reframing of wilderness as discipline, the theology of manna and dependence on God's word, and the spiritual mechanics of prosperity-induced forgetting are covered in the sections above. The notes below guide the group through the passage.)*

---

### Opening note for the leader

It may be worth noting at the start of the session that chapters 7–9 appear to expand on 6:5. Loving the Lord with all heart, soul, and strength looks like: not compromising with the world (ch 7); remembering the Lord's provision even in prosperity (ch 8); and seeking God's righteousness while avoiding rebellion (ch 9–10). This helps the group see the structure and not treat each chapter as a standalone.

---

### 8:1–9 — The Lord Disciplines His Children

**Q1. Why are the Israelites to be careful to follow the Lord's commands?**

So they may live prosperously in the land God has promised — 'live and increase' implies not just survival but genuine flourishing. Obedience is the path to the life God intends for them.

**Q2. Summarise from memory what happened when the Israelites wandered in the desert. Why did that happen? What does it tell us about God's character?**

They grumbled, refused to trust God, and would not enter the land despite seeing what God did to Egypt. God punished them with forty years of wandering until the adult generation died. This was God disciplining his people (v5) — taking sin seriously, but doing so as a father, not as an executioner.

**Q3. How does Moses explain what God was doing during that time? How was it discipline and not simply punishment?**

Humbling them — to rely on him and not themselves. Testing them — to see if they would obey. Teaching them through hunger and manna that life comes from the word of the Lord, not just from food. Protecting them and providing for them for forty years (clothes and sandals did not wear out). The wilderness was a school, not just a sentence.

**Q4. What is the Lord about to do (vv7–9)? How are the Israelites to respond, and why is the reminder of the wilderness so important?**

Bring them into a good land of extraordinary abundance — water, crops, fruit, minerals. They are to obey God and revere him. The wilderness reminder is crucial: they need to know that everything they are about to receive comes from the same God who provided manna. The abundance of the land does not change the source of provision; it just makes it easier to forget.

---

### 8:10–20 — Remember the Lord's Provision and Beware of Prosperity

**Q5. What are the spiritual dangers the Israelites will face when they enter the land?**

- They may forget the Lord
- They may fail to obey the Lord
- They may become so prosperous that they become self-satisfied and proud, and forget that the Lord saved them from slavery and gives them the ability to produce wealth

**Q6. What safeguards does Moses give them?**

A call to remember. A reminder of their helplessness without God's provision. A reminder that it is God who enables them to produce wealth — not their power and strength alone. They must rely on him and not become proud or self-satisfied.

---

### Application Questions

**What was at the heart of the Israelites' sin? How can we show the same attitude?**

Forgetfulness rooted in self-sufficiency. The belief, functional if not stated, that we are the authors of our own success. We show the same attitude whenever we fill our schedule with the gifts and forget to spend time with the giver; whenever we attribute our security to our savings rather than to God; whenever answered prayer produces gratitude for thirty seconds and then business as usual.

**When are we most likely to become proud and self-satisfied? Try to be specific.**

After a professional achievement; when a relationship is going well; when we are healthy; when finances are comfortable; when we are busy with good things. The irony is that the moments most likely to produce pride are often the moments when God's provision is most visible — if we had eyes to see it.

**How can we go about remembering the discipline and provision of the Lord? What has that looked like in the gospel, in Christian history, and in our own experience?**

In the gospel: the cross is the ultimate reminder that our life comes from God's provision, not our own effort. In Christian history: the stories of the church's faithfulness under persecution, the stories of extraordinary provision in missionary work, the witness of saints who found God sufficient in extremity. In personal experience: encourage the group to share specific examples of God's provision that they are in danger of forgetting. Hebrews 12 may be helpful here — see what the group makes of it depending on how the discussion is going.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 9:1–10:11: You Won't Enter the Land Because You Are Better — Rely on Your Mediator

background.body:
**Position in the sermon.** Chapter 9 is the third expansion of the Shema (6:5), completing the triad of chapters 7–9. Loving the Lord with everything looks like: not compromising with the world (ch 7), remembering his provision in prosperity (ch 8), and — most searching of all — abandoning any belief that you deserve what God gives you (ch 9). This final movement is in some ways the most theologically important: it attacks the root of spiritual pride.

**The structure of 9:1–10:11.** The passage moves in three clear stages: a warning against assuming the land is a reward for righteousness (9:1–6); a detailed, humbling rehearsal of Israel's rebellion — especially the golden calf at Horeb — including Moses' extended intercession (9:7–10:11); and the renewal of the covenant and giving of the second tablets (10:1–11). The overall movement is: you are not righteous — you are deeply rebellious — but God is merciful and provides a mediator.

**The golden calf (9:8–21).** The timing of this rebellion is the most shocking element: it happened while Moses was on the mountain receiving the law. Moses presents this not as a historical embarrassment but as a diagnosis of Israel's condition — 'stiff-necked' is the verdict he repeats (9:6, 13).

**Moses' intercession (9:25–10:11).** Moses interceded for forty days and nights, twice (vv18–20, 25–29). His intercession is remarkable: he does not argue that the people are good or deserve forgiveness. He argues entirely from God's character and reputation — 'you brought them out'; 'your people and your inheritance'; 'lest the land from which you brought us say…' This is intercession grounded in God's own commitments, not human merit. The New Testament sees here a type of Christ's intercession (Hebrews 7:25; Romans 8:34).

**The second tablets (10:1–5).** The reissuing of the law after the golden calf is an act of extraordinary grace. God does not give Israel a different or reduced covenant after their catastrophic failure. He gives the same law, renewed.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: you won't enter the Promised Land because you are better than others, so rely on your mediator.** Entry into the land is not a reward for righteousness. It is an act of God's faithfulness to his own promises and his judgment on the Canaanites' wickedness. Israel brings nothing to the table except need.

**'Not because of your righteousness' — one of the most important sentences in Deuteronomy.** The repeated insistence of vv4–6 directly addresses a perennial religious temptation: the assumption that good circumstances reflect our merit. This is rejected here at its root.

**The wickedness of the Canaanites is not Israel's righteousness.** God's judgment on the Canaanites is real and just. But it does not make Israel righteous by contrast. Both are sinful; the difference is election, not virtue.

**Moses foreshadows Christ as mediator.** The parallels are striking: forty days and nights of fasting; standing between God's wrath and sinful people; interceding on the basis of God's character rather than the people's merit; presenting himself as willing to bear God's anger (v19). The New Testament explicitly identifies Jesus as the ultimate fulfilment of this role.

**Grace renews, not just forgives.** The giving of the second tablets is not merely restoration to the status quo. God reissues the covenant and continues to lead. His response to catastrophic failure is full renewal, not grudging reinstatement.

pastorals.body:
**The temptation to self-righteousness by comparison is universal and rarely conscious.** Most people do not explicitly think 'I deserve good things and others don't.' But the instinct is common. This passage is a direct and uncomfortable corrective. It is not a club to beat the group with, but a mirror to look into together.

**Some in your group may be in a period of genuine failure or sin.** The reminder that 'you have been rebellious against the Lord from the day I knew you' (9:24) is not meant to crush — it is meant to redirect toward the mediator. The pastoral move is: yes, you are a sinner; so was Israel; and God provided Moses to stand in the gap. He has provided something far greater for you.

**Moses' appeal to God's reputation (9:28).** This is not manipulation — it is praying in alignment with God's own purposes. The concern for God's reputation is a legitimate motivation in intercession, worth drawing out carefully.

exegetical.body:
**'Stiff-necked' (9:6, 13).** A metaphor from agriculture: an ox stiffening its neck against the farmer's direction. Applied to Israel, it describes wilful resistance to God's guidance — not ignorance but refusal.

**The repetition of forty days and nights (9:9, 18, 25).** Moses fasted for forty days receiving the law, then fasted again for forty days interceding after the golden calf. The New Testament echo in Jesus' forty days in the wilderness (Matthew 4:1–11) connects the pattern: the Son of God reliving and perfecting what Israel failed.

**'Because of your wickedness' (9:27) vs 'because of the Lord's love' (7:8).** These are the two grounds Moses rehearses across chapters 7–9. The land is given not because of Israel's merit but despite their demerit, and not because of the Canaanites' wickedness but because of God's love and faithfulness.

**'At that time' (10:1, 8, 10).** Moses draws together threads from the wider wilderness period to show the completeness of God's grace after the golden calf. The point is not chronological precision but theological fullness: God restored everything.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the group comparing themselves to 'more sinful' people as a way of deflecting the weight of this passage. Moses explicitly forecloses that move: the fact that the Canaanites are wicked does not make Israel righteous. The same move is available to us — 'at least I'm not as bad as...' — and it has the same effect: it redirects attention from our own need for a mediator to a comfortable but false self-assessment. Gently close that door when it opens.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The theology of election vs merit, Moses as type of Christ the mediator, and the pastoral dimensions of confronting self-righteousness are covered in the sections above. The notes below guide the group through the passage.)*

---

### 9:1–6 — Not Because of Your Righteousness

**Q1. What assurance does Moses give the Israelites as they prepare to enter the Promised Land?**

Though the nations they will face are terrifying — including the Anakites — the Lord will cross ahead of them like a devouring fire. He is the one who will destroy and subdue their enemies. The assurance is not 'it won't be that hard' but 'God is going ahead of you.'

**Q2. What wrong thinking might the Israelites fall into?**

That they are entitled to the land, and that God is giving it because of their righteousness — that their good circumstances reflect their own virtue.

**Q3. How does Moses counter this?**

On two fronts: (1) God is judging the Canaanites' wickedness — the land is being taken from them because of their sin, not given to Israel because of their virtue; (2) he reminds them of their own failings — they are 'stiff-necked,' and the golden calf example is about to be rehearsed in detail.

**Q4. Why would this be helpful in teaching the right attitude?**

Just because the Canaanites are wicked does not mean Israel is without sin. The knowledge that God judges wicked nations should make Israel afraid for itself, not self-congratulatory. Both moves — pointing to the Canaanites' wickedness and to Israel's own sin — serve the same purpose: eliminating any ground for pride.

---

### 9:7–24 — Remember Your Rebellion

**Q5. What had their previous rebellion looked like?**

Encourage the group to summarise the golden calf episode with attention to: the timing (this happened at Horeb while God was giving the law — shocking in its brazenness); the content (idolatry after everything God had done); the speed ('you quickly turned aside'); the character (stiff-necked, rebellious); and Moses' role (interceding before an angry God).

**Q6. How did God respond? What was Moses' role?**

The Lord was angry enough to destroy them entirely. Moses was also angry — he broke the tablets — but interceded with the Lord, fasting for forty days and nights.

---

### 9:25–10:11 — Rebellious People Need a Mediator

**Q7. What was the focus of Moses' intercession?**

Not the people's character — Moses has just been describing their catastrophic failure. The intercession is grounded entirely in God's character and commitments: 'They are your people and your inheritance'; 'you brought them out by your great power'; 'remember your servants Abraham, Isaac and Jacob'; 'lest the country from which you brought us say...' This is a masterclass in theocentric intercession.

**Q8. How does the Lord respond?**

He has mercy and does not destroy them (10:10–11). He then commissions Moses to make new tablets — the covenant is renewed in full. God does not give Israel a lesser version of the relationship; he restores everything.

---

### Application Questions

**How does Moses point us to Jesus?**

The parallels are rich: forty days and nights of fasting (echoed in Jesus' wilderness fast); standing between God's wrath and sinful people; interceding on the basis of God's own character; the implication in v19 that Moses was willing to bear God's anger on the people's behalf. Jesus fulfils this pattern perfectly as our great high priest who always lives to intercede (Hebrews 7:25).

**When do we compare ourselves to 'more sinful' people? What does the New Testament say about our righteousness?**

Romans 3:10–12: 'There is no one righteous, not even one.' The movement from 'at least I'm not as bad as...' to 'I stand before God entirely on the basis of Christ's righteousness, not my own' is what this passage is designed to produce.

**How should God's response to Israel's sin shape how we think about our own? When is remembering our rebellion helpful, and when might it become unhelpful?**

Helpful: when it produces humility, drives us to the mediator, prevents complacency, generates gratitude for grace. Unhelpful: when it becomes a spiral of self-condemnation that displaces trust in Christ's intercession. The passage points both directions: take your sin seriously, and look to the one who stands in the gap.

**What does it look like to rely on Jesus as our mediator and remember the covenant he has made with us?**

Specific practices of faith: praying through Christ; receiving communion as a rehearsal of the covenant; confessing sin and trusting forgiveness rather than managing guilt; returning to God's promises when anxiety or failure threatens to overwhelm.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 10:12–11:32: What Does the Lord Ask of You?

background.body:
**Position in the sermon.** This passage forms a transition in Moses' second sermon. Chapters 5–10 have established the theological foundations: the Ten Commandments, the Shema, the three threats to wholehearted love (compromise, prosperity, self-righteousness). Now, in 10:12–11:32, Moses draws together the threads with a comprehensive summary of what the Lord requires — and then sets before the people the stark binary of blessing and curse that will be elaborated in chapters 27–28.

**'What does the Lord your God ask of you?' (10:12).** This is one of the great summary questions of the Old Testament, comparable to Micah 6:8. Moses' answer is not one thing but a cluster of five interlocking responses: fear, walk in his ways, love him, serve him, observe his commands. These are not five separate duties; they are facets of a single life of covenant faithfulness.

**Circumcise your hearts (10:16).** Circumcision was the sign of the covenant — the external mark of belonging to the people of God. Moses uses it metaphorically to point to what the external sign was always meant to signify: a heart genuinely responsive to God. The verb is active — 'circumcise yourselves' — but Deuteronomy 30:6 reveals that ultimately only God can perform this operation. This tension anticipates the new covenant promise of Jeremiah 31 and Ezekiel 36.

**The character of God in 10:17–18.** One of the most concentrated character descriptions in the Old Testament: 'the God of gods and Lord of lords, the great God, mighty and awesome, who shows no partiality and accepts no bribes.' Then immediately: 'He defends the cause of the fatherless and the widow, and loves the foreigner.' The juxtaposition is deliberate — the greatest God pays attention to those everyone else overlooks.

**Blessing and curse (11:26–32).** The section closes with a preview of the ceremony elaborated in chapters 27–28: obedience leads to blessing, disobedience to curse. Moses makes the right choice seem obvious — which makes Israel's subsequent history all the more sobering.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: what does the Lord ask of you?** The answer — fear, walk, love, serve, observe — is comprehensive. It is not asking for something from the religious compartment of life; it is asking for everything, from every dimension, all the time.

**God's greatness and God's compassion belong together.** The God who is 'God of gods and Lord of lords' is the same God who 'defends the cause of the fatherless and the widow.' His greatness is expressed precisely in his attention to the overlooked. His people are to reflect both dimensions.

**'Circumcise your hearts' — the inside must match the outside.** External covenant membership without internal responsiveness is the perennial failure of the religious person. Moses names it and demands the corresponding inward reality. The New Testament reveals this is itself a gift of God's Spirit (Romans 2:29; Colossians 2:11).

**Love for God is expressed through obedience, memory, and care for the vulnerable.** Obedience to his commands (10:13); active remembering of what he has done (11:2–7); care for the foreigner, 'because you were foreigners in Egypt' (10:19). All three flow from genuine love for the Lord.

**The binary of blessing and curse clarifies rather than threatens.** Moses presents the choice starkly not to frighten but to make it plain. There are only two paths. The tragedy is that the clarity did not produce faithfulness.

pastorals.body:
**The comprehensive demand can be overwhelming.** Two responses help: (1) these are not conditions for entering the relationship but the shape of life within it — Moses is describing the life of a people already redeemed; (2) point forward to 30:6, where God promises to circumcise their hearts — the demand and the gift belong together.

**The care for the foreigner (10:18–19) has contemporary resonance.** For those who are themselves immigrants or minorities, the claim that God pays particular attention to the foreigner is deeply personal. For those in majority cultures, the command to love the foreigner 'as yourself' may be more challenging than expected. Handle the contemporary application thoughtfully.

**The blessing-and-curse framework raises questions about God and suffering.** Be prepared for this to arise. A brief response — 'Deuteronomy is setting out a general pattern of covenant life, not a mechanical formula; Job, the Psalms, and the cross deepen and complicate this picture' — will usually be sufficient.

exegetical.body:
**The five imperatives of 10:12–13.** Fear, walk, love, serve, observe are not a hierarchy or sequence but a single complex response to a single great God. They are mutually reinforcing: genuine fear produces the desire to walk in his ways; love expresses itself in service; service is shaped by observing his commands.

**'He chose their descendants after them — he chose you' (10:15).** Election is here described as both historically rooted and personally contemporary. This generation is not inheriting someone else's relationship; they are personally chosen.

**'Remember' (11:2).** The Hebrew is singular — Moses addresses each person individually. Communal rehearsal of what God has done is not a substitute for personal appropriation. Each member is called to make the story of the exodus their own story.

**'The land of Egypt… where you planted your seed and irrigated it by foot' (11:10).** A reference to labour-intensive Egyptian irrigation. The contrast with Canaan (v11) — where rain comes from heaven and God's eye is always on the land — is a contrast between human toil and divine provision. Canaan is a land of dependence; Egypt was a land of technique.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the application of 'what does the Lord ask of us?' becoming a list-management exercise — ticking off fear, obedience, love, service as if they were separate tasks. The point is integration: these are not five duties but one whole-of-life orientation. Push the group toward asking what it would look like for their actual daily life to be shaped by this single orientation, rather than adding religious activities around the edges of an otherwise unchanged life.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The comprehensive covenant summary, the theology of heart-circumcision, and God's care for the vulnerable are covered in the sections above. The notes below guide the group through the passage.)*

---

### 10:12–22 — Fear the Lord and Hold Fast to Him

**Q1. What does the Lord ask of his people? What does each thing mean?**

- **Fear the Lord:** having no greater authority in life than the Lord — his will counts most; fearing nothing more than him
- **Walk in all his ways:** a whole-of-life orientation, not compartmentalised religion
- **Love him:** the Shema again — relationship, not duty
- **Serve him with all your heart and soul:** wholehearted engagement, not passive acknowledgement
- **Observe his commands:** the practical expression of love and fear

These five are facets of one life. Draw out the integration.

**Q2. What rationale is given in vv14–15? What characteristics of the Lord are highlighted?**

The Lord is Creator to whom the whole universe belongs — 'the heavens, even the highest heavens, the earth and everything in it.' And yet he chose Israel and loved them. The contrast is designed to produce awe: the God who owns everything chose to love this small people — solely because of his love.

**Q3. 'Circumcise your hearts' is clearly a contrast to 'stiff-necked.' How does that help us understand what it means?**

Stiff-necked is stubborn resistance to God's direction. Circumcised heart is the opposite: humble, soft, genuinely responsive. It is the inward reality the outward sign of circumcision always pointed to.

**Q4. Why are they to circumcise their hearts? What characteristics of the Lord are given as the reason?**

The greatness and uniqueness of God, and his impartiality. A God this great and this just demands genuine inward response, not just formal membership.

**Q5. What does the Lord's attitude to the poor and marginalised say about his character? What are the Israelites being taught?**

God pays attention to those society overlooks: the fatherless, the widow, the foreigner. They are to reflect God's own character in how they treat the vulnerable — and the ground given is their own experience: 'you were foreigners in Egypt.' Memory of their own vulnerability grounds the call to care for others'.

**Q6. Why should Israel fear and serve the Lord (vv20–22)?**

Because of who he is and what he has done: brought their ancestors to Egypt as seventy people, and made them 'as numerous as the stars in the sky.' The pattern of promise-and-fulfilment is itself a ground for trust and worship.

---

### 11:1–7 — Love the Lord and Remember

**Q7. How is love connected to obedience? What role does remembering play?**

Love for the Lord is expressed in obedience and is itself a response to having seen him at work. They are to remember both his discipline and his mighty acts. The emphasis that 'you yourselves saw' (v7) implies that faithfulness in the next generation depends on this generation passing on what they have witnessed.

---

### 11:8–25 — Obey the Lord and Fix His Word in Your Heart

**Q8. What will obedience produce (vv8–14)?**

Long life in the land, and abundant blessing — rain in season, grain and wine and oil, grass for livestock. This teaches the connection between obedience and dependence: they can obey confidently, knowing God provides abundantly for all their needs.

**Q9. What spiritual dangers will they face? What is the antidote (vv15–25)?**

Being enticed to serve other gods, bringing judgment on themselves. The antidote: immersing themselves in God's word — fixing it on their hearts, binding it on themselves, teaching it constantly to their children — so they stick close to the Lord and do not drift.

---

### 11:26–32 — Make Your Choice

**Q10. What is the choice Moses offers?**

Blessing or curse. Obedience or disobedience. Moses makes the right choice seem obvious — which makes Israel's subsequent history all the more sobering. The clarity of the choice did not guarantee it would be made correctly.

---

### Application Questions
