**What does the Lord ask of us? Go through the different imperatives and discuss what they would look like in your context.**

Encourage the group to work through fear, walking in his ways, love, service, and observance in relation to their specific circumstances — work, family, relationships, money, time. The goal is specificity, not abstraction.

**What spiritual dangers do we face? What is the antidote?**

The dangers Moses identifies — seduction by alternative loyalties, erosion of attention through busyness and comfort — are fully contemporary. The antidote is the same: immersing ourselves in God's word, practising active remembering, building habits and relationships that keep God central.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 12: Worship the Lord in His Place and in His Way

background.body:
**Position in the sermon.** Chapter 12 opens the detailed legal code of chapters 12–26, which elaborates the Ten Commandments and applies them to the specific situations of life in the land. Chapter 12 functions as the introduction to the entire code, establishing the foundational principle: worship must be directed to the right God, at the right place, in the right way.

**One place of worship.** The central command is the requirement to worship at 'the place the Lord your God will choose.' The specific location (which will be Jerusalem) is not yet named. The principle is centrality: unlike surrounding nations whose shrines were scattered across high places, Israel's worship is to be concentrated at a single God-chosen location. This is a radical claim: God determines the terms of his own worship.

**Destroy the existing places of worship.** The command to tear down altars, smash pillars, burn Asherah poles (vv2–3) is the corollary of the positive command. Worship of the Lord cannot coexist with the infrastructure of Canaanite religion. Surviving shrines will become a standing temptation.

**Freedom within the structure.** The chapter is not only prohibitive. It also describes the freedom and joy of covenant worship: bringing offerings and tithes to the place God chooses, eating in his presence, rejoicing — including servants, Levites, foreigners, and the vulnerable (v12). The picture of worship is communal, joyful, and inclusive.

**The application to Christian worship.** The New Testament does not abolish this chapter; it relocates and reinterprets it. Jesus is the new temple (John 2:19–21); worship in spirit and truth replaces geographical centralisation (John 4:21–24). The principle that God determines the terms of his own worship remains fully in force.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: worship the Lord in his place and in his way.** Both elements matter. Not just any worship of the Lord is acceptable — it must be on his terms. This is not arbitrary restrictiveness; it is a statement about the nature of the God being worshipped.

**God defines the terms of his own worship.** Israel is not free to worship God in any way that seems right, adopting the forms and locations of surrounding culture. God specifies. This principle does not diminish worship; it protects it from becoming a projection of human preferences onto God.

**The destruction of existing worship infrastructure is necessary, not merely advisable.** The existing shrines are actively dangerous. They will not be used in new ways for the Lord; they must be demolished. There is no version of Canaanite worship that can be baptised for use in the worship of Israel's God.

**Worship is joyful and communal.** The positive vision of worship is not austere. It includes eating and drinking before the Lord, rejoicing, including everyone in the community — sons and daughters, servants, Levites, foreigners. The worship of the right God in the right way is a feast.

**'You must not worship the Lord your God in their way' (v31).** The practices of Canaanite worship — including child sacrifice — are not just different; they are 'detestable.' The content of worship matters as much as its address. Sincere devotion directed wrongly is not acceptable worship.

pastorals.body:
**'Worship the Lord your God' is not self-evidently simple for everyone.** Some will have complicated relationships with corporate worship — bad experiences with church, doubts about the value of the gathered community. This chapter's insistence that God determines the terms of worship can be both challenging and freeing: you are not left to invent an approach; you are invited into something God has shaped.

**The New Testament application requires care.** The move from 'one place in the land' to 'Jesus as the new temple' is real and important but needs to be made clearly. Help the group trace the logic: the principle (God defines the place and terms of worship) is constant; the application changes in light of Christ. John 1, 2, and 4 are the most useful texts.

**What contemporary idolatry looks like.** Just as Israel was commanded to destroy the surrounding worship infrastructure, the question for Christians is what forms of false worship we are importing into — or allowing to shape — our worship of God.

exegetical.body:
**'The place the Lord your God will choose' (vv5, 11, 14, 18, 21, 26).** This phrase is repeated six times. The location of genuine worship is not the worshipper's choice; it is God's. This resists any version of religion that allows the worshipper to determine their own terms of engagement with God.

**The blood prohibition (vv16, 23–25).** Blood represents life, and life belongs to God. The prohibition is not hygienic but theological: to eat blood is to treat as ordinary what God has declared sacred.

**'Be careful not to be ensnared by inquiring about their gods' (v30).** Moses closes the door even on curiosity about Canaanite worship. Enquiry into false worship as intellectual interest is itself a step toward it. The same caution applies to any form of spiritual tourism.

**Child sacrifice (v31).** Given as the extreme example of Canaanite worship. It functions as a reductio ad absurdum: this is where self-determined worship without God's constraints ultimately leads.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the application discussion drifting into debates about worship style — contemporary vs traditional, musical preferences, liturgy vs informality. Those are real conversations, but they are not what this chapter is primarily about. The chapter is about whether God defines the terms of his own worship, and whether the worship patterns of surrounding culture are being imported unchanged into the people of God. Keep the discussion at that level before it descends into preference debates.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The principle that God defines the terms of his own worship, the positive vision of joyful communal worship, and the New Testament fulfilment in Christ as the new temple are covered in the sections above.)*

---

### 12:1–14 — Worship the Lord in His Way

**Q1. How are the Israelites not to worship? What must they do to avoid idolatry?**

They must not copy the nations. They must destroy all Canaanite worship infrastructure — altars, pillars, Asherah poles, idols. They cannot worship the Lord however or wherever they please.

**Q2. How are they to worship?**

- In the place God shows them (not their own choice)
- In the way God commands them (not their own invention)
- With rejoicing — the positive tone of covenant worship

**Q3. What is the main focus here?**

There is only one legitimate place to worship the Lord. Genuine worship is not self-referential; it is directed to God on his terms.

---

### 12:15–28 — Give to the Lord What Is His

**Q4. What freedom does the Lord give?**

They may eat and enjoy meat from their animals whenever they want, in their own towns, without travelling to the central sanctuary. Not every meal is a religious ceremony. There is genuine freedom for ordinary life.

**Q5. What restrictions are there?**

They must not eat blood. Tithes, firstborns, vowed offerings, freewill offerings, and special gifts must be brought to the place of worship and eaten in the Lord's presence.

**Q6. What is the main point?**

God's laws are not unnecessarily restrictive — there is real freedom in daily life. But in matters of worship, the people must take care to obey: worshipping in his place, in his way, giving what belongs to him.

---

### 12:29–32 — Do Not Worship Like the Nations

**Q7. How does Moses conclude, and why?**

With another warning: do not be ensnared by enquiring how these nations serve their gods. Moses closes that door firmly: do not even ask. And do not add to or subtract from what the Lord has commanded.

---

### Application Questions

**Where do Christians find the one place of worship? What are the implications?**

Jesus is the new temple — the place where God and humanity meet (John 1:14; 2:19–21). Worship 'in spirit and in truth' (John 4:23–24) is worship through Christ and in conformity with the reality he embodies. We cannot approach God except through Christ (John 14:6).

**The Israelites were to destroy all false worship. What might that look like in a Christian context?**

Careful, ongoing discernment: what habits, assumptions, values, and forms of spirituality are we importing into our life with God that belong to a different lord? The seriousness Moses applies — total destruction, no compromise — calibrates how seriously we should take the same discernment.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 13:1–14:21: Loyalty to the Lord Matters — Purge Evil and Be Holy

background.body:
**Position in the code.** Chapter 13 elaborates the first commandment (no other gods) in its most demanding register. Where chapter 12 addressed the external infrastructure of worship, chapter 13 addresses the human agents who might draw people away from the Lord. Chapter 14:1–21 then addresses the external, daily distinctiveness of the people through the food laws.

**Three scenarios of enticement (ch 13).** The chapter presents three escalating cases: a prophet or dreamer who performs signs but leads toward other gods (vv1–5); a close family member or intimate friend who privately tries to lure someone toward idolatry (vv6–11); and an entire town that has been corrupted (vv12–18). The progression maps the way spiritual corruption actually spreads — from individual authority figures, through intimate relationships, to community-wide infection.

**The shock of the language in vv6–11.** The relational language in v6 is notably tender — 'your very own brother, your son or daughter, the wife you love [literally: the wife of your bosom], your closest friend [literally: the friend who is like your soul].' The commands that follow are deliberately devastating: do not yield, do not listen, show no pity, do not spare, do not shield, put them to death. The shock is the point. Moses wants his hearers to be horrified — and to think: 'May this never be me or my family. May we never bring such judgment on ourselves.'

**The stern warning is itself an act of grace.** The warning of chapter 13 is designed to save Israel from the catastrophic consequences of idolatry spreading through the community. 'Purging evil' is not vindictiveness; it is communal health.

**Food laws (14:1–21).** These regulations distinguish clean from unclean animals and prohibit practices associated with pagan mourning (v1). Their primary rationale is stated in v2 and v21: 'You are a people holy to the Lord your God.' The food laws are not primarily about nutrition; they are about daily, embodied covenant distinctiveness.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: loyalty to the Lord matters — purge evil from among you and be a people holy to the Lord.** Holiness is not optional, comfortable, or passive. It demands active, sometimes painful choices, both in what we reject (ch 13) and in how we live (ch 14).

**False teaching is the most serious internal threat to the people of God.** The New Testament is equally firm: false teachers who lead God's people away from the true gospel face eternal condemnation (Galatians 1:8–9). The form of response changes between testaments; the seriousness does not.

**What makes a teacher false is their message, not their credentials.** The prophet in chapter 13 may perform genuine signs and wonders. The test is not 'does this person have impressive gifts?' but 'what do they say about God and his word?' If the message leads away from the Lord, the credentials are irrelevant.

**The most dangerous enticement comes from those closest to us.** The private, intimate enticement of vv6–11 is harder to resist than public false teaching precisely because of the relational bond. Loyalty to the Lord must exceed even the deepest human loyalty. Jesus makes the same demand (Matthew 10:37).

**Holiness is daily and practical, not just liturgical.** The food laws apply at the dinner table, not just at the temple. The people of God are to be distinctive in the ordinary texture of daily life — the same principle that shapes the New Testament call to be 'in the world but not of it.'

pastorals.body:
**Chapter 13 will disturb people — and that is appropriate.** The PDF notes suggest introducing the session by asking whether anyone has experienced false teaching — what it was, what was done, how you distinguish it from legitimate disagreement. That grounds the chapter in concrete experience before the text is read.

**The call to put family members to death is not applicable today — but the principle must not be evacuated.** The New Testament equivalent is church discipline: the removal from the community of those whose teaching persistently leads people away from Christ. That is still costly, still painful, and still necessary. Help the group move from the specific form to the underlying principle without losing the force of the demand.

**The food laws feel remote — help the group engage with the principle.** The specific animal categories require little explanation; the principle they serve — daily, embodied distinctiveness — is entirely transferable. Acts 10 shows how the New Testament handles the transition: the food laws are fulfilled and their specific application ends, but the call to holiness they served is intensified, not abolished.

**Be alert to those for whom the call to resist family enticement is personally live.** Some may be navigating real pressure from family members opposed to their faith, or inviting them toward compromises they know are wrong. Chapter 13 is not an excuse for family rupture, but it speaks directly to the cost of loyalty.

exegetical.body:
**'The Lord your God is testing you' (13:3).** God's permission for a false prophet to perform signs does not mean endorsement. God uses even false teachers to test his people's loyalty. Genuine love for the Lord will hold fast to his word even when impressive phenomena seem to point elsewhere.

**'Purge the evil from among you' (13:5, 11, 17).** This refrain recurs throughout the legal code (17:7; 19:19; 21:21; 22:21; 24:7). The people of God cannot be passive in the face of sin threatening to corrupt the community. The community as a whole bears responsibility for its own holiness.

**'The wife of your bosom' / 'the friend who is like your soul' (13:6).** Moses chooses the most emotionally charged relationships to make the point as sharp as possible: no human bond is an exception to the loyalty owed to God.

**'Ceremonially unclean' (14:7–20).** The clean/unclean distinctions operate as a system of signs marking the boundary between Israel and the nations. When Jesus declares all foods clean (Mark 7:19) and Peter receives the vision of the sheet (Acts 10), the sign-system is fulfilled — but what it pointed to (holiness, belonging to God) remains.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the discussion of false teaching staying entirely theoretical — naming historical heresies or obvious cults, while avoiding the harder question of subtle contemporary distortions. The most dangerous false teaching is not outright denial of Christ but the quiet displacement of him: teaching that adds requirements for spiritual maturity, focuses on leaders or methods rather than Christ, or implies other believers are spiritually deficient. Ask specifically: does this teaching centre everything on Jesus and his cross, or does it offer something extra alongside or instead of the cross?

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The escalating structure of enticement scenarios, the principle that message outweighs credentials, and the theology of daily covenant distinctiveness are covered in the sections above.)*

---

### Introductory exercise

Before reading, ask: Has anyone experienced what they would describe as 'false teaching'? What was it? What was done? How do we know the difference between false teaching and something Christians might legitimately disagree about? This grounds the session in concrete experience.

---

### 13:1–18 — Purge the Evil from Among You

**Q1. Who might lead God's people astray? What is their message? How does this differ from earlier temptations?**

- A prophet or dreamer performing signs (vv1–5)
- A close friend or family member (vv6–11)
- A whole town (vv12–18)

In each case: 'Let us follow other gods.' The difference from chapters 7 and 12 is source: the danger now comes from within the community, not from external Canaanite worship.

**Q2. Why might the Israelites be tempted to listen?**

- The prophet may perform signs and wonders that seem to legitimate his message
- The family member is someone deeply loved
- The Lord may be allowing this to test their loyalty

The temptation is sophisticated and emotionally powerful. Help the group feel the realism of the scenario.

**Q3. How is the message described? What does that tell us about God's attitude to idolatry?**

Rebellion, evil, detestable, condemned. God takes the sole worship of him with the utmost seriousness. Idolatry cannot be tolerated — it is a cancer that, left unchecked, destroys the whole community.

**Q4. How are they to respond, and why?**

The false prophet is put to death for inciting rebellion. Even the most intimate family member or friend is shown no mercy — because the attempt to lead someone away from the Lord is the deepest possible harm. The severity functions as deterrent and as measure of what idolatry costs.

**Q5. How were Moses' original hearers meant to feel? What actually happened?**

Shocked, and determined to avoid idolatry at all costs. In reality, Israel ignored the warning — tolerated idolatry, was drawn into it, came under judgment repeatedly, and ultimately went into exile. Idolatry proved cancerous.

**Q6. What is the cost of staying true to the Lord?**

Possibly the rupture of the most intimate human relationships. But loyalty to the Lord takes precedence over all other loyalties.

**Q7. How do these principles apply to us as New Testament believers?**

The Lord still takes false teaching seriously — more so, given the full revelation of Christ (Galatians 1:8–9). Consider how close friends or family, or apparently miraculous signs, might lead people away from the true gospel today.

---

### 14:1–21 — Be a People Holy to the Lord

**Q8. What are the Israelites forbidden to do? What reason is given?**

Cutting or shaving themselves for the dead (pagan mourning); eating ceremonially unclean animals, birds, or sea creatures; eating animals found dead. Primary reason: v2 and v21 — they are to be holy to the Lord, his treasured possession.

**Q9. What clues suggest the food laws are not simply about health?**

The category of 'ceremonial uncleanness' (not physical dirtiness); the fact that unclean carcasses may be sold to foreigners — the distinction applies specifically to the covenant people, not to food as such.

**Q10. What reason is given for the food laws?**

v2 and v21: they are to be a people holy to the Lord. The laws are about whether God's people will obey him even in daily details, and whether they will be noticeably different from the nations around them.

---

### Application Questions

**Read 1 Corinthians 5:1–13; Galatians 1:8–9; 2 Timothy 2:14–3:9; 2 Peter 2; 1 John 4:1–6. What are false teachers like in the New Testament? What is to happen to them?**

In the New Testament, false teachers face eternal condemnation, not merely physical death. But the response is similarly decisive: they are to be removed from the community. The form changes; the seriousness does not.

**The food laws no longer apply (Acts 10:10–16), but we are still called to be distinctive. What does this look like for us?**

The call is not to a different dietary system but to a different way of life — in sexual ethics, financial practice, treatment of the vulnerable, truth-telling, relating to enemies. The food laws were a daily, embodied reminder of belonging; our distinctiveness takes different forms but serves the same purpose.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 14:22–16:17: Trust in the Generous Lord Results in Radical Generosity

background.body:
**Position in the code.** This section elaborates the eighth commandment (do not steal) and its positive counterpart — generosity — through laws about tithes, the sabbatical year, firstborns, and the three annual festivals. The connecting thread is the practice of trusting God's provision so completely that generous giving becomes natural rather than costly.

**The tithe (14:22–29).** The tithe in Deuteronomy has a notably joyful character. In a normal year, the tithe is eaten by the worshipper and household at the place of worship — a feast of rejoicing before the Lord. Every third year it is given locally to Levites, foreigners, orphans, and widows. The purpose is twofold: to teach the people to fear the Lord by placing his provision at the centre of economic life, and to care for those with no land-based income.

**The sabbatical year (15:1–18).** Every seventh year, debts are to be cancelled and Hebrew slaves freed. The legislation is economically radical: it prevents permanent accumulation of debt-based poverty. Moses anticipates the temptation of v9 — withholding generosity as the sabbatical year approaches — and forbids it. The spirit of the law must not be gamed.

**The three festivals (16:1–17).** Passover/Unleavened Bread, Weeks, and Tabernacles constitute the annual cycle of remembering what God has done: redemption from Egypt (Passover), harvest blessing (Weeks), and wilderness provision (Tabernacles). Each is characterised by rejoicing, remembering, and including the vulnerable. 'Do not appear before the Lord empty-handed' (v16) frames generosity as integral to worship.

**Generosity as trust made visible.** The consistent structural argument across all these laws: God provides abundantly; therefore you can give generously; and giving generously is itself a form of trust — acting as though God will continue to provide. Withholding is the practical expression of not trusting him.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: trust in the generous Lord results in blessing and radical generosity.** Generosity is not an add-on to faith; it is an expression of it. Withholding is not prudence; it is distrust.

**God's generosity grounds Israel's generosity.** The festivals rehearse what God has done — brought them out of Egypt, blessed the harvest, sustained them in the wilderness — and each ends with giving. Because the Lord has been lavish with us, we can be lavish with others. Generosity flows from gratitude, not from surplus.

**The sabbatical year is economically radical and deliberately so.** The cancellation of debts and freeing of slaves restructure the community's economic relationships. The goal — 'there need be no poor people among you' (15:4) — is an achievable covenantal reality if the people obey. The poor and vulnerable are built into the architecture of Israel's economic life, not treated as an afterthought.

**'Give generously… without a grudging heart' (15:10).** Moses addresses attitude as much as action. Generosity that is technically compliant but internally resentful misses the point. The New Testament makes the same point: God loves a cheerful giver (2 Corinthians 9:7).

**The festivals integrate worship and social life.** Rejoicing at the festivals is not a spiritual activity separate from concern for the poor — it explicitly includes Levites, foreigners, orphans, and widows at the table. A worship that excludes the poor is not the worship Moses describes.

pastorals.body:
**Money and generosity are among the most personally sensitive topics in any group.** Approach application with genuine humility rather than prescription. The goal is not to induce guilt but to help people examine the relationship between their financial practice and their trust in God.

**The question of who the poor and vulnerable are in your specific context.** The laws are consistently attentive to the Levite, foreigner, orphan, and widow — those without land, income, or family network. Your group will have their own version of these categories. Help them be specific: who are the economically vulnerable within reach of your community?

**The sabbatical year raises questions about systemic justice that can feel overwhelming.** Avoid arguing for specific political positions. Help the group see that the text calls for a disposition and practice of generosity beginning in their own sphere of influence — and to consider what communal, not just individual, generosity might look like.

exegetical.body:
**'So that you may learn to revere the Lord your God always' (14:23).** The purpose of the tithe is primarily spiritual formation, not redistribution. Bringing a tenth of everything to the place of worship, and eating it there, is a regular enacted reminder that everything belongs to the Lord. The practice shapes the attitude.

**'There need be no poor' (15:4) vs 'There will always be poor' (15:11).** These are in apparent tension. The first describes the covenant ideal: if the people obey, poverty within the community can be minimised. The second is a realistic description of the ongoing fallen condition. Jesus quotes the second (John 12:8). Both are true and both remain applicable.

**'Do not be hard-hearted or tight-fisted towards your poor brother' (15:7).** The word 'brother' (*'ach*) is the same word used for intimate family in chapter 13. The obligations of generosity are familial, not merely civic: treat a fellow community member in poverty as a member of your own household.

**Passover in Deuteronomy (16:1–8) vs Exodus 12.** Deuteronomy permits cattle as well as sheep and goats, and requires celebration at the central sanctuary rather than in individual homes. These modifications reflect the settled-community context of Deuteronomy. The theological substance — remembering redemption from Egypt — is identical.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the discussion of generosity staying at the level of individual giving and missing the communal and structural dimensions of this passage. The sabbatical year is not an individual act of charity; it is a restructuring of economic relationships within the community. The festivals are not private devotion; they are communal feasts that explicitly include the marginalised. Push the group toward asking what generous, structurally attentive care for the vulnerable looks like in the life of your specific community, not just in personal budgeting.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The connection between trust, obedience, and generosity; the economic radicalism of the sabbatical year; and the integration of worship and care for the vulnerable in the festivals are covered in the sections above.)*

---

### 14:22–15:23 — Radical Generosity

*(Worked through in small groups using a table: for each law, what is commanded, why, who benefits, and how it demonstrates trust in the Lord.)*

**Key observations to draw out in feedback:**

**Tithe (14:22–29):** In most years a feast of rejoicing at the place of worship; every third year redistributed to the Levite, foreigner, orphan, and widow. Trust expressed: the giver trusts that the Lord will provide even after a tenth is given away.

**Sabbatical year — debt cancellation (15:1–11):** Every seventh year, debts among Israelites are cancelled. Moses anticipates (v9) the temptation to withhold generosity as the year approaches and forbids it. Trust expressed: the lender trusts that the Lord will provide even if the debt is never repaid.

**Sabbatical year — freeing of slaves (15:12–18):** After six years, Hebrew slaves are freed — and sent away with generous provision. Motivation: 'Remember that you were slaves in Egypt and the Lord redeemed you' (v15). Trust expressed: the owner trusts the Lord to provide the labour they need without permanently enslaving their neighbours.

**Firstborn animals (15:19–23):** The firstborn of herds and flocks belong to the Lord — eaten at the place of worship with rejoicing. Trust expressed: giving the first to God declares that all increase comes from him and will continue to come from him.

---

### 16:1–17 — Rejoicing in the Lord's Blessings

**Q2. Summarise how the Israelites are to celebrate each festival. What are they to remember?**

- **Passover/Unleavened Bread:** sacrifice an animal, eat unleavened bread for seven days, remember the escape from Egypt
- **Weeks:** seven weeks after harvest begins, bring a freewill offering, rejoice — including servants, Levites, foreigners, orphans, and widows — and remember they were slaves in Egypt
- **Tabernacles:** after threshing and winemaking, rejoice for seven days with the whole community including the vulnerable, remembering the abundance the Lord has given

**Q3. What do all these festivals have in common? What do they say about the character of the Lord?**

All emphasise rejoicing and remembering: redemption from slavery, arrival in the Promised Land, abundant blessing. The Lord is generous, present, and worthy of celebration — and the celebration is always communal and inclusive.

**Q4. How does this relate to the call to radical generosity in chapter 15?**

They respond to the Lord's generosity with rejoicing and by being generous themselves. Constantly remembering God's provision gives freedom and motivation to give. Gratitude, not guilt, drives the giving.

---

### Application Questions

**How are we called to steward our resources as New Testament believers?**

2 Corinthians 8–9 is the richest extended New Testament treatment: giving that flows from knowing the grace of Christ, is voluntary and cheerful, and trusts that God will provide. The sabbatical year logic — structuring generosity into the rhythm of life — finds its echo in regular, proportional, joyful giving (1 Corinthians 16:2).

**How, as Christians, are we to relate to the poor and vulnerable? Who are the poor and vulnerable among us?**

Encourage the group to name specific people and situations. The biblical pattern is not general concern for abstract poverty but particular attention to specific people within reach. The Deuteronomic vision of a community where no one is permanently destitute is a challenge and a goal, even if it cannot be fully achieved before the new creation.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 16:18-18:22: God's People and Their Leaders Must Live Under God's Word

background.body:
**Position in the code.** This section addresses the institutions of leadership in Israel: judges and courts (16:18-17:13), kings (17:14-20), priests and Levites (18:1-8), and prophets (18:9-22). Together these three offices -- king, priest, prophet -- constitute the structures through which God governs his people in the land. The section's unifying principle is that every leader, without exception, must live under God's word. Power is never autonomous in Israel.

**Judges and justice (16:18-17:13).** The administration of justice is presented as a communal responsibility: the people themselves are to appoint judges, and those judges must be impartial, incorruptible, and thorough. Cases too difficult for local courts are referred upward. The death penalty for contempt of court (17:12) underlines how seriously the system of justice is to be taken -- it is not merely civic but theological, a reflection of God's own justice.

**The king (17:14-20).** The law of the king is among the most extraordinary texts in the ancient Near East. Every other contemporary law code assumes that the king is the source of law and stands above it. Deuteronomy inverts this entirely: the king is to write out the law himself, keep it with him, read it all the days of his life, and not consider himself better than his fellow Israelites. He must not multiply horses, wives, or wealth -- the three classic routes to autonomy from God and exploitation of the people. The king is a servant of the law, not its master.

**Priests and Levites (18:1-8).** The Levites have no land inheritance because the Lord himself is their inheritance. They are to be supported by the people so they can fulfil their ministry. This is a statement about what the community values -- the service of God and his word is worth sustaining materially.

**Prophets (18:9-22).** In contrast to the divination and occult practices of surrounding nations, Israel is to listen to the prophet the Lord raises up. The test of a genuine prophet is simple: does what they say come true? A false prophet is to be put to death. The section anticipates 'a prophet like Moses' (18:15, 18), a figure the New Testament identifies as Jesus (Acts 3:22; 7:37).

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: God's people and their leaders must live under God's word if they are to thrive in the land the Lord is giving them.** No office, however exalted, places its holder above Scripture. This is the most counter-cultural claim in the whole section.

**Justice is a theological, not merely civic, concern.** The insistence on impartial, thorough, incorruptible justice reflects the character of God himself. Where justice is perverted in the community of God's people, it is not just a social failure -- it is a theological one.

**The king must be the most avid student of the law.** The requirement to write out the law personally and read it every day is the structural safeguard against the abuse of power. The three prohibitions -- no multiplication of horses (military power), wives (political alliances and the idolatry they bring), or gold (personal wealth) -- target the three main routes by which ancient kings achieved autonomy from God.

**The prophet greater than Moses points forward to Christ.** The promise of 18:15-18 is one of the great Messianic texts of Deuteronomy. The New Testament is unanimous that this prophet is Jesus -- the one who speaks with divine authority, not as a mouthpiece but as the very Word of God (Acts 3:22; 7:37; John 1:1).

**All three offices -- prophet, priest, king -- find their fulfilment in Christ.** Jesus is the prophet who speaks God's word with ultimate authority; the priest who offers himself as the final sacrifice and intercedes for his people; the king who rules in perfect submission to the Father. This is the richest application point of the session.

pastorals.body:
**The law of the king is profoundly counter-cultural and may land differently depending on who is in your group.** Those who have experienced the abuse of power -- in workplaces, families, or churches -- may find the insistence that no leader stands above God's word deeply reassuring. Those in positions of leadership may find it personally searching. Both responses are appropriate.

**The leaders of God's people in Jesus' day were disobeying these commands.** The scribes and Pharisees multiplied legal requirements; the chief priests pursued wealth and political influence; the ruling council operated to serve their own interests. Jesus' conflicts with the religious leadership are illuminated by this background. Help the group see the connection without turning it into a generalised critique of all religious authority.

**The question of what it means to 'listen to the prophet of God today' requires careful handling.** Some in your group may be in contexts where prophecy is claimed freely and the test of 18:22 is rarely applied. Others may be in contexts where prophecy has been dismissed entirely. Help the group think about what it means to listen to the word of God through Scripture, preaching, and the community of faith -- without resolving all the questions about contemporary prophetic gifts.

exegetical.body:
**'Follow justice and justice alone' (16:20).** The Hebrew is emphatic: *tsedeq tsedeq tirdof* -- 'justice, justice you shall pursue.' The doubling is rhetorical intensification. Justice is to be the defining orientation of the community's legal life, not a secondary value.

**'He must not return the people to Egypt to get more horses' (17:16).** Egypt was the great source of horses and military hardware in the ancient Near East. A king who builds military power through Egyptian horses is not only acquiring weapons -- he is rebuilding dependence on the power that enslaved Israel. The prohibition is simultaneously military, economic, and theological.

**'A prophet like me from among your own brothers' (18:15).** The identification 'like me' (*kamoni*) is significant: this prophet will share Moses' role as mediator of God's word but will surpass him. Acts 3:22 and 7:37 apply this explicitly to Jesus; John's Gospel presents Jesus as the Word of God who speaks with the Father's own authority throughout.

**'If what a prophet proclaims does not take place or come true' (18:22).** The test is falsifiability: a genuine prophet's words will prove true. This is a warning against treating every claimed prophecy as authoritative. The test must eventually be applied.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the application of 'listen to the prophet of God' collapsing into a discussion of contemporary prophecy debates, which can become absorbing but tangential. The more important question for most groups is: what does it look like to listen to Christ -- the prophet greater than Moses -- through his word, in the gathered community, and in the power of his Spirit? Keep the focus on that before secondary questions consume the session.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The theological significance of the king-under-law, the fulfilment of all three offices in Christ, and the pastoral dimensions of leadership accountability are covered in the sections above.)*

---

### 16:18-17:13 -- Prioritise Justice Among the People of God

**Q1. What are the leaders among the people of God to be like? Who is responsible for making sure justice is upheld?**

Judges are to judge fairly, without partiality or bribery, following 'justice and justice alone.' The people themselves bear responsibility -- they are to appoint just judges. Justice is a communal responsibility, not just a governmental one.

**Q2. How is sin to be dealt with among the people of God?**

- Rumours of wrongdoing must not be ignored
- Accusations must be carefully and thoroughly investigated -- don't act on unsubstantiated hearsay
- Matters are to be dealt with publicly
- No one is to be put to death on insufficient evidence -- two witnesses are required, and they must be involved in the sentence
- Cases too complex for local resolution must be referred to higher authority
- Leaders must be respected; contempt of court is treated as a capital offence

**Q3. What does this tell us about the character of God? How can these principles be applied among God's people today?**

God is a God of justice who protects the innocent and judges the guilty. He wants his people to be characterised by thoroughness, honesty, and incorruptibility. Contemporary application: the principles of evidence, due process, public accountability, and referral of complex cases to appropriate authority remain directly transferable to church life and beyond.

---

### 17:14-18:22 -- Kings, Priests, and Prophets

**Q4. What restrictions are placed on future kings? How are they to rule? Why might horses, wives, or wealth turn them from the Lord?**

The Lord appoints the king; he must not multiply horses (military power through Egyptian alliance), wives (political alliances that bring foreign gods), or gold (personal wealth and the independence it creates). Most importantly: the king writes out the law himself, keeps it with him, reads it every day, learns it, reveres it, and follows it -- not considering himself better than his fellow Israelites. Trade with Egypt might rebuild dependence on the power that enslaved them; wives lead to idolatry (as with Solomon); wealth encourages self-sufficiency rather than reliance on the Lord.

**Q5. How are the people to relate to the priests of the Lord?**

To support them materially so they can fulfil their ministry. The Levites receive no land inheritance -- the Lord is their inheritance -- and depend on the community's support.

**Q6. Which voices are God's people to listen to, and which must they not listen to?**

They listen to the prophet of the Lord who speaks God's word. They must not practise divination or other occult practices. They must assess whether a prophet is genuine by whether their words come true; false prophets are not to be feared and are to be put to death.

---

### Application -- Jesus: True Prophet, Priest, and King

**Q7. What was Jesus' experience with the leaders of God's people? How were they disobeying these commands?**

The scribes and Pharisees multiplied legal requirements and used religion for status (Matthew 23). The chief priests pursued wealth and political power (Mark 11:15-17; John 11:47-53). The ruling council acted to protect their own position. Jesus' conflicts with religious leadership are consistently framed in Deuteronomy's terms: leaders who placed themselves above the law rather than under it.

**Q8. How does Jesus fulfil the roles of prophet, priest, and king?**

- **Prophet:** Jesus speaks with authority that surpasses Moses -- 'You have heard that it was said… but I say to you.' He is not merely a mouthpiece; he is the Word (John 1:1). Acts 3:22 and 7:37 apply Deuteronomy 18:15 directly to him.
- **Priest:** Jesus offers himself as the final and perfect sacrifice, and 'always lives to intercede' for his people (Hebrews 7:25). His sacrifice never needs repeating (Hebrews 9-10).
- **King:** Jesus rules in perfect submission to the Father -- the king who lives most completely under God's word. His kingship is exercised through service and sacrifice, not accumulation (Philippians 2:5-11).

**Q9. What does it mean to listen to the prophet of God today?**

Listening to Christ the prophet means: submitting to his word as recorded in Scripture; hearing him through preached word in the gathered community; receiving his teaching through the Spirit who leads us into all truth (John 16:13). It means applying the test of 18:22 to any claimed word from God -- does it accord with what has already been revealed in Christ and Scripture?

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 19:1-22:8: Trust the Lord, Protect the Vulnerable, and Punish the Guilty

background.body:
**Position in the code.** This large section continues the elaboration of the Ten Commandments, moving through laws that expand on 'you shall not murder' (ch 19-21) and related concerns about the sanctity of life, the conduct of war, and the protection of the vulnerable. The unifying principle is the value God places on human life -- made in his image, belonging to him, not to be disposed of lightly by anyone.

**Cities of refuge (19:1-13).** The cities of refuge protect those who kill accidentally from cycles of blood revenge, while ensuring deliberate murderers cannot use them as a shield. The system is sophisticated: it distinguishes motive (accidental vs premeditated), requires investigation, and ensures the avenger of blood acts legitimately only for deliberate murder. Law here serves both mercy and justice simultaneously.

**False witnesses (19:15-21).** The extension of the murder laws into the prohibition of false testimony reflects the recognition that judicial murder -- condemning an innocent person through lying -- is as serious as physical murder. The lex talionis ('eye for eye') in this context is a principle of proportionality: the false witness bears exactly the penalty they tried to impose on the innocent.

**War (20:1-20).** The regulations for warfare are remarkable for what they assume: the battle belongs to the Lord, not to Israel's military strength. The exemptions from service (vv5-8) -- new house, new vineyard, new wife, afraid -- serve the theological point that God does not need a large army. The distinction between distant cities (offer peace first) and nearby cities (destroy utterly) reflects the principle of chapter 7: the nearby nations are a specific spiritual threat.

**Protection of the vulnerable (21:1-23).** The laws of chapter 21 consistently extend protection to those with least power: the victim of an unsolved murder (the whole community must atone); the captive woman (given time to mourn, treated with dignity); the firstborn of an unloved wife (his inheritance rights protected); the rebellious son (the community, not a potentially cruel father, applies the penalty).

**Value life in all its dimensions (22:1-8).** The cluster of laws in 22:1-8 extends care to animals, property, and the safety of others (the parapet law for rooftops). God's people cannot treat anything in his creation with careless indifference.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: trust the Lord, protect the vulnerable, and punish the guilty.** These three imperatives flow from the same source -- a God who values every human life, administers justice without partiality, and calls his people to reflect his character in communal life.

**God cares about the details of justice.** The precision of the laws -- two witnesses required, the false witness bearing the penalty they sought to impose, cities of refuge calibrated to motive -- reflects a God who is not satisfied with rough approximations. Careless administration of justice is not a minor failing; it is a failure to reflect the character of God.

**The battle belongs to the Lord.** The exemptions from military service and the priestly exhortation before battle make the same theological point: Israel's military success does not depend on the size or quality of its army. God is the one who fights. This is an argument for reliance, not passivity.

**Sin has communal dimensions.** The atonement required for an unsolved murder (21:1-9) reflects the principle that innocent blood defiles the whole community. The community bears corporate responsibility for maintaining the conditions of justice and purity.

**God values life in its full breadth.** The laws of 22:1-8 -- returning a neighbour's lost animal, building a parapet, not taking a mother bird with her eggs -- show that covenant holiness extends to the treatment of animals, property, and the physical environment.

pastorals.body:
**The laws about warfare will generate questions about the ethics of holy war and modern military conflict.** Be prepared to acknowledge them without being derailed. The more immediately transferable points are: God does not require our strength or numbers; fear of the enemy is a reasonable human response God accommodates; the exemptions reflect a God who values the whole person.

**The law of the captive woman (21:10-14) is one of the most difficult passages in this section.** In its ancient context it is protective -- requiring the Israelite to treat a captive woman with dignity, give her time to grieve, and forbidding her sale or enslavement if he later loses interest. It should not be romanticised and is not a model for contemporary practice, but it should be understood in its historical context before being assessed.

**The rebellious son (21:18-21) can feel shocking.** Note that the parents do not execute the son -- the community does, after a public process in a small town where everyone would know if the father was speaking the truth. The principle is about the communal cost of persistent, wilful rejection of all authority -- not a template for family discipline.

exegetical.body:
**'Eye for eye, tooth for tooth' (19:21).** In its original legal context, lex talionis is a principle of proportionality and limitation -- the punishment must fit the crime and must not exceed it. It prevents escalating vengeance. Jesus' reinterpretation in Matthew 5:38-42 does not contradict the original principle; it deepens it by calling his followers to absorb wrong rather than insisting on their legal rights.

**'The Lord your God goes with you' (20:1, 4).** The priestly exhortation before battle is a liturgical act. Going to war is a theological event in Israel, not merely a military one. The priest speaks, not the general.

**The parapet law (22:8).** Rooftops in the ancient Near East were living spaces. A roof without a parapet was a genuine danger to visitors. The law reflects the principle that property ownership carries responsibility for the safety of others.

**'Hang on a tree' (21:22-23).** Exposure of an executed criminal's body was a sign of divine curse. Paul quotes this in Galatians 3:13: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us, for it is written: "Cursed is everyone who is hung on a pole."' The requirement to bury the body before nightfall adds to the theological freight Paul draws on.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the group spending the session cataloguing the difficulties of the text -- the captive woman, the rebellious son, the warfare regulations -- without arriving at the session's main questions: what does God care about according to this passage, and whose responsibility is it to make sure the innocent are protected and the guilty punished? Acknowledge the difficulties honestly but keep moving toward the application.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The theology of justice as a reflection of God's character, the theological framing of warfare, and the consistent protection of the vulnerable are covered in the sections above.)*

---

### 19:1-21 -- Prioritise Justice and Protect the Innocent

**Q1. Summarise the main situations referred to in chapter 19. How do they expound 'you shall not murder'?**

Three scenarios: accidental manslaughter (cities of refuge protect those who kill without intent); deliberate murder where the killer tries to hide behind accidental status (elders investigate and hand them to the avenger of blood); false testimony intended to frame a neighbour (judicial murder). Together they show that the prohibition of murder encompasses not just the act but every attempt to deprive an innocent person of life -- whether by violence, deception, or manipulation of the legal system.

**Q2. What is God's priority in all these situations? How is justice to be done in each case?**

Protecting the innocent while punishing the guilty -- simultaneously. Cities of refuge protect the accidentally guilty; the exception clause ensures the deliberately guilty cannot hide there. Two witnesses are required; malicious false testimony is treated with the utmost seriousness. The dual concern reflects a God who does both mercy and justice at the same time.

**Q3. What do vv19-20 tell us about the character of God?**

God protects the innocent and judges the guilty. He wants his people to reflect that -- characterised by compassion, justice, and honesty. The deterrent purpose ('the rest of the people will hear and be afraid') shows that justice shapes the moral culture of the whole community, not just the individual case.

---

### 20:1-20 -- Waging War as the People of God

**Q4. How are leaders to encourage fearful soldiers? How might vv5-9 reinforce trust in God?**

Reminder of what God has done and that he is the one fighting for them. The exemptions allow those with unfinished personal business or paralysing fear to go home -- a theological statement that God does not rely on large, perfectly committed armies. If he can win with a smaller, less fearful force, the victory is more clearly his.

**Q5. How are nearby cities to be treated differently from distant ones? Why?**

Distant cities: offer peace first; if they refuse, besiege and kill the men but spare the rest. Nearby cities: destroy everything. The nearby nations are a specific spiritual threat; distance reduces the risk of contagion, proximity requires total separation.

**Q6. What do vv19-20 tell us about God's priorities?**

The land and its fruit trees are not to be destroyed in siege warfare -- they are God's creation producing food, not enemies. This reflects God's care for the created order and a practical wisdom: you cannot live in a land you have made uninhabitable.

---

### 21:1-23 -- Looking After the Land with Fairness

**Q7. How are the people to deal with an unsolved murder? Why is this a community matter?**

The elders of the nearest town perform an elaborate ritual of atonement -- a heifer, a declaration of innocence, a prayer. Sin defiles the community and the land. The whole community must respond to innocent blood even when the killer is unknown. Corporate responsibility extends to maintaining the conditions in which justice is possible.

**Q8. Who is being protected in vv10-17? Why? How is each protected?**

- **Captive woman (vv10-14):** Given a month to mourn, treated as a wife rather than a slave, cannot be sold or enslaved if her husband later loses interest.
- **Son of the unloved wife (vv15-17):** The firstborn's inheritance rights must be honoured even if his mother is not the favoured wife. The law prevents sentiment or preference from overriding justice.

**Q9. Why is persistent rebelliousness in a son not to be tolerated (vv18-21)?**

The execution is carried out by the men of the town -- providing a communal check on parental abuse. The principle is that wilful, persistent rejection of all authority is socially contagious and must be addressed publicly.

**Q10. What was the problem with leaving a body exposed on a pole overnight?**

A sign of divine curse -- a public declaration of rejection that would defile the land. Paul draws on this directly in Galatians 3:13 to explain the significance of Christ's death.

---

### 22:1-8 -- Value Life

**Q11. What values hold the laws of vv1-8 together?**

All of them value God-given life and care -- animal life, human safety, the wellbeing of others, the integrity of creation. The people of God cannot treat anything in God's creation with careless indifference.

---

### Application Questions

**What does God care about according to today's passage?**

Justice -- precise, impartial, protecting the innocent and punishing the guilty. Life -- human life in all its vulnerability, animal life, the created order. The vulnerable -- the captive, the accident victim, the firstborn of an unloved wife. The community's corporate responsibility for maintaining these values.

**Whose responsibility is it to make sure the innocent are protected and the guilty punished? How can this be applied in our time?**

In Deuteronomy it is communal -- the people appoint just judges, the elders perform atonement for unsolved crimes. In a modern context: the responsibility of citizens to support just institutions; of churches to practise just internal governance; of every member of the community to refuse to be a passive bystander to injustice.

**What did Israel need to know when fearful? What about us?**

That the Lord their God goes with them and fights for them -- he does not need their strength or numbers. For us: the same God is with us in Christ -- 'I will never leave you or forsake you' (Hebrews 13:5, quoting Deuteronomy 31:6).

**Are there ways in which today's passage points forward to Jesus?**

Most directly, 21:22-23: Christ became a curse for us, hung on a tree, so that the blessing of Abraham might come to the Gentiles (Galatians 3:13-14). The cities of refuge point forward to Christ as the one to whom those under the threat of judgment can flee and find safety.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 22:9-23:14: Maintain the Boundaries That Resist Sin and Keep God's People Holy

background.body:
**Position in the code.** This section extends the code's concern for holiness into the domains of sexual relationships, communal boundaries, and the holiness of the camp. It continues the theme present since chapter 12: the people of God are to be distinctively shaped by God's word in every area of life, not just in formal worship.

**The mixed regulations of 22:9-11.** The prohibitions against mixing different kinds (different seeds in a vineyard, ox and donkey ploughing together, wool and linen woven together) reflect the principle of maintaining the natural and moral order established by God. They frame the section that follows on sexual boundaries: just as different kinds must not be confused in creation, so the distinctions God has established in human sexuality must not be confused or violated.

**Sexual laws (22:13-30).** This extended section deals with cases involving sexual honour, marriage, and rape. The consistent direction of the law is toward the protection of women: from false accusation (vv13-19), from being treated as guilty when they are victims (vv25-27), and from destitution through violation (vv28-29).

**The assembly of the Lord (23:1-8).** The regulations about who may 'enter the assembly of the Lord' have their primary concern in the purity of the worshipping community. The permanent exclusion of Moabites and Ammonites reflects their historic hostility; Edomites and Egyptians may eventually be included. Crucially, the story of Ruth the Moabite -- welcomed into Israel and becoming an ancestor of David and Jesus -- is a significant corrective to any rigid reading of these laws.

**The holiness of the camp (23:9-14).** The requirement for ritual cleanliness in the military camp is grounded in v14: 'the Lord your God moves about in your camp.' The physical regulations are a theological statement about the holiness appropriate to the presence of God.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: maintain the boundaries that resist sin and keep God's people holy.** Holiness requires boundaries -- not arbitrary ones, but boundaries that reflect the character of God and protect those who would otherwise be vulnerable.

**God protects the vulnerable, especially in sexual ethics.** The consistent direction of the sexual laws is toward the protection of women -- from false accusation, from the consequences of rape, from abandonment after violation. The more powerful party bears greater legal responsibility for the more vulnerable.

**The assembly of the Lord has boundaries -- and those boundaries will ultimately be expanded by Christ.** The exclusions in 23:1-8 are not the final word. Isaiah 56:3-8 promises foreigners and eunuchs will be welcomed into God's house. Ephesians 3 and Galatians 3:26-29 declare that in Christ there is neither Jew nor Gentile. The boundaries of the Old Testament assembly pointed toward a purity now achieved through faith in Christ.

**The presence of God requires holiness.** The regulation of the military camp is grounded in the theological fact that God is present there. The New Testament equivalent: the indwelling Spirit means the believer's body is a temple (1 Corinthians 6:19-20), with all the implications that follow.

pastorals.body:
**The sexual laws in this section deal with experiences some in your group may have lived through -- false accusation, rape, sexual violation, betrayal.** Approach this section with care and awareness. The laws are ancient and their specific applications do not transfer directly, but the values they embody -- that sexual violation is treated with the utmost seriousness, that the vulnerable are protected, that abusers bear legal and communal consequences -- are potentially healing for those who have experienced the opposite.

**The exclusions in 23:1-8 can feel troubling.** Hold the historical specificity and the scriptural trajectory together: these laws had a specific covenantal function; the story of Ruth and the prophecy of Isaiah 56 show that God's purposes were always broader than these regulations might suggest.

**Purity and holiness can be received in very different ways.** For those with a background in legalistic religion, the language of purity can trigger guilt and shame. Help the group locate holiness in its right place: not as a condition of acceptance by God, but as the appropriate response of those who are already accepted, living in the presence of the holy God.

exegetical.body:
**The mixing prohibitions (22:9-11).** The rabbis understood these as reflecting the principle that God has established distinct categories in creation, and that mixing them dishonours the Creator's ordering. The connection to the sexual laws that follow is intentional: the same principle (respect for God-given distinctions and boundaries) applies in both domains.

**'The Lord your God moves about in your camp' (23:14).** This is the theological key to the whole camp-purity section. The regulations about bodily waste are not about sanitation; they are about the holiness appropriate to God's presence. This logic is taken up in 1 Corinthians 6:19: 'your bodies are temples of the Holy Spirit' -- with corresponding implications.

**'Neither Jew nor Gentile' (Galatians 3:28).** Paul's declaration that ethnic and social distinctions of the old covenant have been overcome in Christ is the New Testament resolution of the assembly boundaries in 23:1-8. The purity of the assembly is now achieved through union with Christ -- available to all, without distinction of ethnicity, physical status, or national history.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the discussion of the sexual laws in chapter 22 becoming either clinically detached (treating the laws as historical curiosities) or suddenly and awkwardly personal if someone in the group has lived through a related experience. Hold the space carefully: these passages deal with real human pain and real human dignity. The leader's job is to keep the theological focus -- what does God value here, and who is he protecting? -- while being pastorally attentive to those for whom the text is not abstract.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The protective direction of the sexual laws, the trajectory from exclusion to inclusion in the assembly of the Lord, and the theology of holiness grounded in God's presence are covered in the sections above.)*

---

### 22:9-30 -- Keep Relationships Pure

**Q1. What do the instructions of vv9-11 have in common? How might v5 fit in? Why are they here?**

Maintaining the natural order and distinctions God has established -- not allowing different kinds to be confused or mixed. Verse 5 maintains the God-given distinction between male and female. Together these introduce the section on sexual boundaries: the same principle (respect for God's established distinctions) applies in both domains.

**Q2. What different scenarios are presented in 22:13-30? What repeated ideas do you notice?**

- Man falsely accuses wife of not being a virgin: he is publicly punished and may never divorce her; if the accusation is true, she is put to death
- Adultery (consensual): both die
- Rape of a betrothed woman in the city: both die (she is presumed complicit if no cry was heard)
- Rape of a betrothed woman in the country: only the man dies; the woman is treated as a victim
- Rape of an unbetrothed woman: the man must marry her and cannot divorce her
- A man must not marry his father's wife

The repeated phrase 'purge the evil from among you' signals that sexual sin is a communal matter requiring communal response.

**Q3. What measures are taken to protect the vulnerable?**

The falsely accused wife is protected by public punishment of the husband and prohibition of divorce. The rape victim in the country is explicitly distinguished from the adulteress -- she bore no guilt. The unbetrothed woman violated is given permanent material security through the compulsory marriage (a social safety net in a world where a non-virgin had very limited marriage prospects).

**Q4. What do these laws say about God and what he wants for his people?**

God cares about the details of sexual ethics -- protecting the weak, treating violation with full seriousness, placing responsibility on those with power. He does not want sin to take root and spread in the community.

---

### 23:1-14 -- Keep the People of God Pure

**Q5. What is being protected in 23:1-8? How and why?**

The purity and integrity of the worshipping community. The Moabites and Ammonites are permanently excluded because of their hostility to Israel on the journey from Egypt. Edomites and Egyptians may eventually be included -- after three generations. Note: Ruth the Moabite was welcomed into the people of God and became the ancestor of David and Jesus -- a significant counter-example to any rigid ethnic reading.

**Q6. What is the reason for the laws in vv9-14? What do they teach?**

The Lord is present in the camp -- 'the Lord your God moves about in your camp.' Nothing sinful or unclean can be permitted where God is present. The physical regulations about bodily waste are a theological statement, not a hygiene instruction: God's holiness demands appropriate holiness from his people in every dimension of life.

---

### Application Questions

**How is the teaching to 'purge the evil from among you' applied in the New Testament? Read 1 Corinthians 5.**

Paul applies the same logic to the Corinthian church: sexual immorality within the community must not be tolerated; the man must be removed from the fellowship. The reason is the same as Deuteronomy's: sin is like leaven -- it spreads through the whole lump (1 Corinthians 5:6). The goal of discipline is the restoration of the individual and the protection of the community.

**How does the coming of Jesus change who can be included in the people of God? See Isaiah 56:3-8; Ephesians 3; Galatians 3:26-29.**

Isaiah 56 explicitly promises that foreigners and eunuchs -- both excluded by Deuteronomy 23 -- will be welcomed into God's house. In Christ, the boundaries of the old assembly are transformed: the criterion is faith in Christ, not ethnicity or physical status. The wall of hostility has been abolished (Ephesians 2:14-16). This is not a contradiction of Deuteronomy but its fulfilment.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 23:16-25:19: Do Justice and Love Mercy

background.body:
**Position in the code.** This section covers a wide range of laws primarily elaborating the eighth commandment (do not steal), the ninth (do not bear false witness), and the tenth (do not covet), together with regulations protecting the vulnerable and upholding honesty in economic life. The unifying thread is what it means for the people of God to be characterised by justice and mercy in the everyday texture of economic and social life.

**Asylum for runaway slaves (23:15-16).** The requirement to give asylum to a runaway slave is extraordinary in its ancient context. Every other ancient law code required the return of escaped slaves and imposed penalties for harbouring them. Deuteronomy inverts this entirely. The reasoning is implicit but powerful: Israel was itself a nation of escaped slaves. To return a runaway slave is to undo the logic of the exodus.

**Various laws on honesty and exploitation (23:17-24:7).** These laws consistently target the exploitation of the vulnerable by the powerful: the prohibition of cult prostitution; the ban on charging interest to a fellow Israelite; the requirement to fulfil vows promptly; the right of a worker to eat from the field they are working; the prohibition of kidnapping. Kidnapping receives the death penalty (24:7) -- the strongest possible statement of what it means to steal a person's freedom.

**Protection in social and economic relationships (24:8-25:4).** A cluster of laws extending protection to borrowers, workers (wages the same day), parents and children (each dies for their own sin -- no collective punishment), foreigners, orphans, widows (leave gleanings in the field), and even working animals (do not muzzle an ox while it is threshing). The reach of God's concern is comprehensive.

**Honest weights and measures (25:13-16).** The prohibition of different weights for buying and selling is grounded in a striking theological statement: 'the Lord your God detests anyone who deals dishonestly.' Commercial dishonesty is not a minor technical infraction; it is detestable to God.

**The Amalekites (25:17-19).** The section closes with the command to blot out the memory of Amalek -- those who attacked the weakest Israelites from the rear on the journey from Egypt. This is the dark counterpart to the section's persistent concern for the vulnerable: those who exploit the weak face the ultimate judgment.

keyPoints.body:
**The big idea: do justice and love mercy.** This is Micah 6:8 before Micah. The laws of this section flesh out what justice and mercy look like in the grain fields, the courts, the marketplace, and the family -- in the everyday fabric of economic and social life.

**God sees and cares about economic and social exploitation.** The consistent attention of these laws to power asymmetries -- creditor and debtor, employer and worker, citizen and foreigner, able-bodied and vulnerable -- reflects a God who notices what the powerful do to the powerless.

**Honest dealing in all its forms is a theological matter, not just an ethical one.** The statement that the Lord 'detests' dishonest weights is a claim about God's character. Dishonesty in commerce is not a secular matter; it is a sin against the God who values truth and fairness in every domain.

**The memory of Egypt grounds the call to justice.** The most frequently given motivation for care of the vulnerable is: 'remember that you were slaves in Egypt, and the Lord your God redeemed you' (24:18, 22). The experience of vulnerability and redemption is the ground from which justice and mercy grow.

**Even working animals deserve care.** 'Do not muzzle an ox while it is treading out the grain' (25:4). Paul quotes this in 1 Corinthians 9:9 to argue for the right of gospel workers to be supported materially. God's concern for fair treatment extends to the animal world.

pastorals.body:
**This section is rich in application but dense in detail.** The PDF study guide suggests a structured group exercise -- going through a section of the laws and asking what is being 'stolen' in each case -- which is a productive approach. It keeps the group in the text rather than jumping too quickly to generic application.

**The asylum law for runaway slaves is worth dwelling on.** It is genuinely counter-cultural in its ancient context and remains counter-cultural today. The logic of the exodus -- that God redeems the enslaved and requires his people to extend that logic outward -- has powerful contemporary implications in discussions of migration, asylum, and the treatment of those fleeing oppression.

**Commercial honesty may feel like a minor issue compared to the 'big' moral questions.** Help the group see that Deuteronomy does not share that instinct. Dishonest weights and measures, wage theft, exploitation of debtors -- these are treated with the same seriousness as the laws about murder and idolatry. The partition between 'serious' sins and 'minor' economic failings is not a biblical one.

exegetical.body:
**'You shall not hand over to their master a slave who has escaped to you' (23:15).** This is a direct inversion of the norm in every other ancient Near Eastern law code. Hammurabi's Code required the death penalty for anyone who harboured a runaway slave. Deuteronomy's radical departure reflects the logic of the exodus: the God who rescued slaves cannot require the return of those fleeing slavery.

**'Each is to die for their own sin' (24:16).** One of the clearest statements of individual moral responsibility in the Old Testament. It sits in productive tension with passages about generational consequences (Deuteronomy 5:9): collective consequences do not negate individual accountability.

**'Do not muzzle an ox while it is treading out the grain' (25:4).** Paul quotes this twice (1 Corinthians 9:9; 1 Timothy 5:18) as a principle that those who work deserve to be paid -- applying it to the financial support of apostles and elders. The original law and Paul's application share the same underlying principle: it is unjust to benefit from someone's work and withhold appropriate return.

**'What Amalek did to you on the way' (25:17).** The Amalekites attacked Israel from the rear -- targeting those who were 'tired and weary' and 'lagging behind.' This cowardly exploitation of the weakest is the precise antithesis of everything the legal code has been requiring.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the application discussion staying at a comfortable level of generality -- 'yes, we should be honest and care for the poor' -- without pressing into specific and personal territory. The most useful intervention is to ask: where specifically, in your actual economic and social life this week, do these laws have something to say? Wages, debts, borrowing, lending, treatment of employees, commercial honesty -- these are not abstract categories. Push gently toward the concrete.

studyQuestionsAndNotes.body:
*(The theology of justice and mercy grounded in the memory of Egypt, the comprehensive reach of God's concern, and the treatment of commercial honesty as a theological matter are covered in the sections above.)*

---

### Opening exercise

This section expounds the commandments 'do not steal,' 'do not bear false witness,' and 'do not covet.' In groups, go through 23:16-24:7 and ask: what is being stolen in each case? What reasons are given for these laws? What values do they teach?

Key observations:
- **23:15-16:** The runaway slave's freedom is not to be 'stolen back' -- exodus logic applied outward
- **23:17-18:** Cult prostitution steals worship from God and dignity from the person
- **23:19-20:** Interest on loans steals from the poor by profiting from their need
- **23:21-23:** Breaking a vow steals what was promised to God
- **23:24-25:** A hungry worker may eat from a field but not take in bulk -- the right to eat is honoured; the right to profit at another's expense is not
- **24:1-4:** Laws on divorce protect women from being passed back and forth as commodities
- **24:6:** Taking a millstone as collateral steals a person's ability to produce food -- their livelihood
- **24:7:** Kidnapping -- enslaving a fellow Israelite -- receives the death penalty; the strongest possible statement of what it means to steal a person's freedom

**What do you notice about 24:7? What does it tell us about God's view on enslaving a fellow human being? Why would the law in 23:15-16 have been so surprising at the time?**

Kidnapping for slavery is placed in the same category as murder in the weight of its penalty. The asylum law of 23:15-16 is not merely humanitarian but theological: returning an escaped slave is an offence against the God who freed slaves.

---

### 24:8-25:4 -- Justice and Mercy in Social and Economic Life

**What is commanded? What motivations are given? Who does the Lord care about?**

- **24:10-13:** Do not enter a borrower's house for collateral; return a poor person's cloak before nightfall -- 'it is all they have to sleep in'; the Lord will see it and credit it to you as righteousness
- **24:14-15:** Pay wages the same day -- the poor worker is counting on them
- **24:16:** Each dies for their own sin -- individual accountability
- **24:17-22:** Do not deprive the foreigner or the orphan of justice; leave gleanings for the foreigner, orphan, and widow -- 'remember that you were slaves in Egypt'
- **25:1-3:** Flogging must not exceed forty stripes -- 'do not degrade your fellow Israelite'; even punishment has a limit
- **25:4:** Do not muzzle an ox while it is threshing -- even the animal deserves the fruit of its labour

The Lord cares about the borrower, the worker, the foreigner, the orphan, the widow, the accused, and the working ox. The reach of his concern is total.

---

### 25:5-19 -- Levirate Marriage, Honest Weights, and the Amalekites

**What do the laws in 25:5-12 concern? Why are they seen as important?**

The duty of a surviving brother to marry his deceased brother's widow, preserving the family name and inheritance. The woman is protected from destitution. The man who refuses is publicly shamed -- communal accountability for care of the vulnerable.

**What motivations are given in 25:13-16? What does this tell us about God's character?**

'The Lord your God detests anyone who deals dishonestly.' Commercial dishonesty is personally offensive to God, who values truth and fairness in every domain. The motivation for honesty is God's character, not merely the risk of being caught.

**Why does today's passage end as it does (25:17-19)?**

Amalek attacked the weakest Israelites from the rear. The command to blot out their memory is the dark counterpart to the whole section's concern for the vulnerable: those who exploit the weak -- especially the weakest -- face the most severe judgment.

---

### Application Questions

**What principles are there for God's people today?**

The memory of our own redemption grounds generosity toward the vulnerable; God sees and cares about economic exploitation; honesty in commerce is a theological matter; individual accountability before God; the comprehensive reach of justice and mercy from the court to the grain field.

**What does it mean to love justice and mercy as God's New Testament people? Can you think of practical examples?**

Encourage the group to name specific situations: paying fair wages; not exploiting flexible workers; honest dealing in all commercial transactions; leaving 'gleanings' -- whatever that looks like in your context -- for those with less; supporting those in debt without profiting from their need.

**In what areas could this passage be a challenge to Christians today?**

The laws about wage theft, debt, and honest weights may be more personally searching than laws about murder or idolatry for many in the group. The partition between 'spiritual' and 'economic' matters is not a biblical one.

chapters.title: Deuteronomy 26-28: Worship, Obey, and Heed the Warning of the Covenant Curses

background.body:
**Position in the sermon.** Chapters 26-28 form the conclusion of Moses' second sermon. Chapter 26 provides the liturgical bookend to the legal code -- just as the code opened with the call to worship in the right place and the right way (ch 12), it closes with a liturgy of offering and remembrance. Chapters 27-28 then set out with stark clarity the covenant consequences of the two paths available to Israel: obedience brings blessing; disobedience brings curse.

**The liturgy of the firstfruits (26:1-15).** The worshipper bringing the firstfruits is to recite a condensed version of Israel's salvation history -- the wandering Aramean ancestor, the descent into Egypt, the slavery, the cry, the redemption, the land. This is not merely a prayer; it is a theological identity statement. The worshipper places themselves within the story: what God did then is the ground of what they owe now.

**The covenant identity (26:16-19).** The section closes with a mutual declaration: Israel declares that the Lord is their God; the Lord declares that Israel is his treasured possession. This is the covenant in miniature -- a relationship of mutual belonging, loyalty, and obligation.

**The ceremony on Ebal and Gerizim (ch 27).** The elaborate public ceremony of blessing and curse is a communal act of covenant ratification.
